name: Michael Olsen
markdown:
Bridging Innovation and Infrastructure:
Standardizing Geospatial Data for Smarter
Transportation Projects

Michael J. Olsen

00

1

You are what you eat…

• Cheap food->poor diet->not sustainable
• Moderation->good diet->sustainable
• Luxury dining–>expensive->not

sustainable. Special occasions only.

Food decisions affect quality of life!!

Thanks to David Koppe

Your decisions/designs are as good as how well you survey…

• Cheap surveys-> poor design->
unsustainable infrastructure with
high maintenance costs and safety
issues

• Moderate surveys->good designs-

> sustainable infrastructure

• Luxury surveys–>expensive->not
sustainable for all projects, not
able to use all data downstream.
May make sense for special
situations.

Survey decisions affect quality of life!!

Thanks to David Koppe

Show me the money-ROI
•Difficulty in measuring ROI due to

decentralized tracking.

•Importance of safety and risk
mitigation in ROI calculations.
•Benefits are obvious, why do we

need to compute it?

•Benefits of NSRS modernization:
enhanced positional accuracy,
improved data integration, and
interoperability.

High ROI now and later: 3D Engineered Models,
Automated Machine Guidance, Mobile lidar
• 3DEM + AMG

• Results in much more precise construction
• Bids were approximately $480,000 lower
• Estimated ROI (2,269%) and B/C ratio (23.69),
• Estimated 416 x higher odds of a Pass on grade

verification

• Mobile lidar collected across state on a

biannual basis (12,880 km)

• Reduced field labor and fuel costs
• Decreased congestion
• >2:1 ROI based on present value analysis.
• Expected to increase with more usage

• Mobile lidar retro-reflectivity

• AASHTO Supplemental Research Award 2023
• Savings from use of tools on one project paid

for research costs

Jahanger et al. (2023), Martin et al. (2020), Olsen et al. 2018

3DEP

• Estimated return on investment of 5:1

• Annual budget of ~$36M

• Benefits estimated to be ~$690 million per annum

• Potential to increase to $1 billion if fully funded for completion

within 8 years

• Standardized and authoritative 3DEP data is available free of

license or restricted use

• An envisioned 3DEP Ecosystem concept would govern and connect
elevation resources owned and managed by multiple agencies
under a single user interface

What is the value of the confidence and peace of
mind associated with being able to TRUST data?

Standards are like a toothbrush.

Everyone agrees that we need them,
but no one wants to use anyone else’s.

Gaps and Limitations

• Note that Standards, Guidelines, and Technology were removed.

Limitations of Current Standards

Outdated Standards -
Many standards were
developed decades ago and
haven’t kept up with
advancements in technology,
especially with new GNSS and
geospatial tools.

Complexity and Length -
Some guidelines are overly
lengthy and challenging to
apply quickly in the field.

Insufficient Focus on Practical
Applications - Many guidelines
emphasize high-accuracy control
surveys but don’t address data for
asset lifecycle management or less
stringent operational needs.

Lack of Consistency -
Different state DOTs follow
different standards, resulting
in a lack of uniformity across
regions.

Insufficient Error
Standards - Limited or
unrealistic error budgets for
new methods (e.g., kinematic
GNSS), and no clear standards
for certain data quality checks.
Need for Centralized
Standards - A desire for unified,
nationwide standards to improve
consistency, reduce confusion,
and support operational
efficiency.

Slow Adaptation to
Technology - Guidelines
and standards often fail to
keep pace with rapidly
evolving geospatial
technology, limiting new
tools like lidar and sUAS.

Inadequate Guidance on
Responsibilities -
Ambiguity in roles between
surveyors, mappers, and
data collectors, and a lack
of standard operating
procedures for non-
GPS\GNSS tools.

Development of a surveying and
mapping guide for transportation
(NCHP 08-174)

00

11

Research Team

Michael Olsen
PI, Transpo
Geospatial Apps.

Christopher Parrish
UAS, Photogrammetry
& Bathymetric Surv.

Yelda Turkan

Implementation

Transportation

Geodesy

& Geodetic Control

Surveying

Surveying & Data
Management

Valerie Lam
Data Scientist

Dimitrios Bolkas
Surveying & Geodesy

Hongtao Dang
Geospatial ROI & DBE

Gene Roe
Digital Innovation
& Standards

NCRHP 08-174 Project Panel

NCHRP Senior Program Officer: Jennifer Weeks

Panelist

Agency

Wei Johnson, Chair

South Carolina DOT

Nick Dutil

Maine DOT

Curtis Clabaugh

ESP Associates, Inc.

Brett Wood

Florida DOT

Hagen Beckstead

Idaho DOT

Rachel Lewis

Jason Wang

Lindsay Riley

Ohio DOT

Appalachian Regional Commission

Utah Department of Transportation

Michael Dennis

National Geodetic Survey

Matthew Corrigan

FHWA Liaison

Objective

Create a surveying and mapping guide:

•

Identify positional accuracies of geospatial data used in
transportation projects by state agencies and others

• Clearly specifies practices consistent with open data standards,

the NSRS, and the deprecation of the U.S. survey foot.

• Help agencies establish an appropriate level of accuracy for a

given application

• Provide details of practices that, if widely adopted, could ensure
geospatial data are reliably and efficiently captured, shared,
and reused.

Motivation • The 2021 Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act (IIJA)

requires the U.S. DOT to promote, implement, deploy,
demonstrate, showcase, support, and document the
application of advanced digital construction management
systems (ADCMS) to enhance project execution.

• The National Spatial Reference System (NSRS)

modernization (2026)

• New State Plane Coordinate System
• New Horizontal Datum (Dynamic).
• New geopotential (vertical) datum.

• NIST and NGS deprecated use of the U.S. survey foot on

December 31, 2022.

• The international foot has been adopted for all applications
throughout the United States, including the modernized
NSRS.

• The U.S. survey foot is currently used by 44 states.

Motivation
• NGS is modernizing the national spatial reference

system.
- once in a generation opportunity

• From survey to geospatial information provider

• Proper geospatial data management
• Staggering data volumes – IT support
•
• Requires trust
• Need a unified, integrated 3D transportation data model

"Collect Once - Use Many" to support ROI

• Considerations

• Change is hard
• 2D paper to 3D digital models
• From design & construction to asset managers
• Built the interstate highway system

Scope of
“The Guide”

• Define a set of common terminology;

• Consistently characterize, document, and report positional accuracy

to maximize interoperability independent of specific manufacturers,
vendors, products, or software;

• Ensure data is captured, shared, and reused by all stakeholders with

confidence, transparency, and efficiency;

• Support future lifecycle and business uses such as building information
modeling (BIM) for infrastructure, digital twins, and asset performance
prediction and modeling;·

• Discuss the overall return on investment (ROI) of positional accuracy and

alignment with the NSRS;

• Present a ROI methodology that calculates the benefits and business
case for positional accuracy and migration to the modernized NSRS;

• Provide recommendations for specific applications appropriate to

various phases and business uses in the lifecycle of an asset or project;

• Consider the benefits and risks of data repurposing and use of

geospatial data throughout the asset lifecycle;

•

•

Include applications that consider both the spatial and temporal
characteristics of the data; and

Include a companion recommendation commentary to the guide that
provides a succinct section-by-section analysis of the background,
rationale, importance, and benefit of each recommendation.

name: William Wing
markdown:
Lidar and Lawmen: DOCUMENTING
HISTORICALLY SIGNIGICANT
SITES WITH MODERN
TECHNOLOGY

By Will Wing and Arizona Young Surveyors Network

Tombstone History

• Ed Schieffelin: American Prospector(1847-1897)

• Founded Tombstone, AZ in 1879 after starting the largest

silver boom in the Southwest.

• Ed filed his first mining claim on September 21, 1877

“Tombstone”

• Friends described Schieffelin as “About the strangest

specimen of human flesh I ever saw. He was 6 feet 2 inches
(1.88 m) tall and had black hair that hung several inches
below his shoulder and a beard that had not been trimmed
or combed for so long a time that it was a mass of unkempt
knots and mats. He wore clothing pieced and patched from
deerskins, corduroy and flannel, and his hat was originally a
slouch hat that had been pieced with rabbit skin until very
little of the original felt remained."

• “The only thing you’ll find out there is your tombstone.”

•

In 1876, Schieffelin and his party were attacked by Apaches,
and a man named Lenox was killed.

• By 1879 their two best mines averaged 50k per month

(approx. $1.5 million in today’s value)

Wyatt Earp – survey crew_”flag man”

Wyatt Earp – flagman & surveyed his own mine

Doc Holiday – signed affidavit of improvements

The Courthouse

• Became the center of the newly
formed Cochise County (1881)

• Original Courthouse was a

wooden building that
succumbed to fire in June of
1881

• Current Courthouse was built in

1882

• AZ became a state in 1912 and

this building served as the
courthouse until 1929 (moved
to Bisbee)

• Became the second state park
in Arizona in 1959 (first to
open)

• Beautiful museum today.

The Arizona Young Surveyors Network

• Established (formally) in 2023 as a committee of the Arizona
Professional Land Surveyors Association (APLS) and National
Society of Professional Surveyors (NSPS)

• Became an official Chapter of AZPLS in 2024 with a seat at the

Board of Directors.

• AZYSN is First and foremost a networking/educating opportunity

for young professionals.

• Wanted to put together fun and exciting outings where we can
give back to the community while sharing our knowledge and
educating the next generation.

Boundary and Legal
Document Research

• Boundary and Legal Document Search using

the Cochise County GIS Database and County
Recorders Website.

• GLO Research starting with the Mineral

Surveys (Mining Claims) as they were there
before AZ was a US State and had section
corners.

• Print out the GLO Plats and research the BLM
Survey Note index to find the notes that
relate to our Section, Township, and Range.

• Read through the notes to find out what was

set and look for topo calls that will help in the
search.

• Once the section corners are verified the
parcel maps can be tied to these found
monuments.

• AutoCADD Civil3d is then used to draft all the
records and calculate search coordinates for
fieldwork at the courthouse property.

FIELDWORK

• Setting up our GNSS Base

and getting checked in to NGS
Control Point “COMSTOCK”
and the highest point in
Tombstone.

• Locating the Section Corners
in Section 11 Township 20
South Range 22 East in order
to get tied in to the Plat
geometry. Used GLO notes
from 1910 to identify the
Stone markers.

• Section corners on Township

Exteriors were GLO stones set
in 1881. The Subdivision was
done in 1910 and GLO stones
were again set.

Collecting
Evidence

• Once we got the property oriented to

the geometry of the plat, we were able
to search and find almost all the
property corners of the courthouse.

• Some of them had to be beeped up

using a metal detector and then dug out
with a shovel.

• A couple of them were in the middle of
the street.  Luckily, this is not a very
busy street.

• Using an NGS point as our Control Point
put our project on to the Arizona State
Plane Coordinate System utilizing NAVD
88 for our vertical datum.

• All Points were recorded in the data

collector and on paper notes in a field
book.

3D LiDAR SCANNING

• Control Points are set in a manner to collect as much
of the building as possible per setup. We already had
good control from the boundary so now we just set
intermediate CP’s to use for scanning the building.

• The beauty of the Trimble SX12 is that it has the 3D
LiDAR Scanner built into the total station, so this
assures that all your scans will already be
georeferenced into the project control. This saves a
lot of time-consuming post processing steps.

• The scans are collected at each setup and shown in

the 3d map screen of the TSC7 Data Collector.  Each
scan shows up as a different color and allows you to
see your coverage and decide what is enough.

3D LiDAR SCANNING

Photogrammetry

• Setting Ground Targets.

• Checking the Weather

Report.

• Complete Pre Flight Check

List.

• Complete the Flight Plan and

set flying height at
appropriate level.

• Fly Project and Download all

photos on to lap top.

• Run the processing software
to compute the orthomosaic
photo.

Processing Data in Trimble
Business Center

• Download the .JXL file and all associated Scan Files

from the Data Collector and create a new job in Trimble
Business Center.

• Drop the .JXL into the TBC project and let it colorize the

point cloud.

• Drop the Orthomosaic Photo into the TBC Project.

• Classify the Point Cloud in TBC and analyze your data

and look for any objects that may have been classified
incorrectly.  Fix the issues and save your project.

• Export the drawing and or Point Cloud to the format

your client is requesting.

•

If Topo is needed, you can pull points from the point
cloud and export to surface drafting software.

Museum Artifacts

THANK YOU FOR ATTENDING OUR PRESENTATION.
WE ABSOLUTELY LOVE THIS PROFESSION AND ITS AN HONOR TO
SHARE IT WITH THE NEXT GENERATION!

Please feel Free to ask any questions after our last speaker.

Happy National Presidents Day!!!
summary: This document focuses on documenting historically significant sites using modern technology such as lidar. It details the process of setting up control points and collecting evidence for the historic courthouse in Tombstone, AZ, followed by 3D lidar scanning to accurately capture its structure.

In his professional career, Chad has successfully led teams conducting

Why do we need single photon technology?

− High altitude, high point density, high
productivity, and limited laser power

− Minimize the laser output required for detection

− Single-Photon Avalanche Diode (SPAD)

detectors are far more sensitive than Avalanche
Photo Diode (APD) detectors used in linear-
mode LiDAR systems

− Output from a single laser pulse can be split to
illuminate multiple locations on the ground,
each illuminating an individual detector element

Advances in remote sensing technology

Better… faster… cheaper…

Increase resolution:  Higher point density, smaller GSD

Increased swath widths: Higher scan angles, larger footprintsr

So what are the implications?

Do we still need overlap?

3

Projects for Analysis

3 projects site were selected to represent gradient of forest canopy densities

•
•

Sparse and medium forest sites from Colorado front range (Arapaho)
Dense forest site from Washington coast range (WADNR)

4

Acquisition Overview

3 acquisitions were used in this analysis.  Sensor and acquisition parameters will influence results.
Some discussion on sensor characteristics is provided but quantitative comparisons were made within single acquisitions.

Pre-Analysis Data Treatment

To isolate influences of swath overlap and point density, datasets were
decimated to replicate single line coverage and low density.

•

Original data: 50% overlap
Pulse decimated: Every other pulse removed w/ 50% overlap maintained
Swath decimated: Every other swath removed w/ full pulse density in swath maintained.

5m cross sections illustrating decimation for WA coast range site (dense forest)

Decimation and Density

Swath decimated data have roughly the same native ground densities after decimation.

*Dual sensor was flown for single line coverage so not swath decimated

7

Goals of swath overlap for forestry

• Comprehensive coverage of site with multiple look angles to minimize occlusion of

canopy and ground.

• More consistent native point distribution.

• More consistent average scan angles throughout site influencing both intensity and

vertical point distribution.

8

Analyses

3 categories of derivatives relevant to forestry applications were generated
and analyzed to assess significance of lidar overlap.

Ground Density: Distribution of ground classified lidar returns influencing
resolution and detail of terrain models.

Intensity: Spectral intensity of lidar first returns.  Used for classification into broad
species groups (conifer/deciduous) and health assessment.

Vertical Point Distribution: Metrics quantifying distribution of lidar returns
throughout the canopy.

9

Ground Density
Voids in ground model defined by
interpolation threshold of 5m between
ground points.

Ground Density-
Pre decimation: 1.25 (ppsm)
Pulse decimated:  0.82 (ppsm)
Swath decimated:  0.86 (ppsm)

Void Area-
Pre decimation: 3.01% of area
Pulse decimated: 6.26% of area
Swath decimated: 20.61% of area

*Effect is more significant in dense
forests than sparse.

10

Intensity

Intensity is influenced by scan angle,
range, slope, and target size hit

Single swaths are more affected by
localized conditions and viewing
geometry

Overlap provides multiple perspectives
that help stabilize intensity
normalization

11

Canopy cover

Original data (top) has highest canopy
cover.

Swath decimated data has flightline
artifact of lower cover (SW->NE)
compared to point decimated.

Point decimated is consistent but lower
than original data.

*Effect is more significant in dense
forests than sparse.

12

Vertical Point Distribution

Scan angles influences canopy penetration and thus vertical point distribution metrics.
50% overlap data has more consistent average scan angles with nadir pulses from 1 line
being averaged with high angle points from adjacent.

Plot illustrating the
bimodal distribution
of height percentiles
of the swath
decimated (reduced
overlap) dataset.

13

Vegetation Summary

Perhaps the most significant artifact of single line
coverage is the data gaps seen in the derivatives.

14

Conclusions

Overlap is valuable for forestry applications in lidar.

Overlap creates more consistent point density, intensity, and distribution throughout the
canopy and ground than single coverage.

Overlap is more important in dense forests than sparse forests due to canopy occlusion.

Overlap will not create perfect normalization of point distribution or intensity.

*Additional analyses recommended to standardize sensors, acquisition parameters, and
forest types.

*Other applications of lidar (particularly ground feature resolution and individual tree
segmentation) are also likely to benefit from overlap due to better more consistent
penetration to ground and comprehensive coverage of canopy.

15

Thank you… Questions?

Mischa Hey- mischa.hey@nv5.com

16

name: Christian Stallings
markdown:
Overcoming Stability Challenges
with Aerial LiDAR Wing Pods

Christian Stallings, Vertical Aspect
Mantas Vaskela, AISPECO

The Rise in Popularity
of Wing Pods

Lower cost, greater flexibility, broader access

•

External mounting with approved STCs

• No aircraft modification required

• Compatible with fixed-wing and helicopters

•

•

Enables lower-cost aircraft (C172, C182, R44)

Faster mobilization and reduced operating costs

The Stability Tradeoff

Aircraft motion limits consistent LiDAR coverage

• Aerial mapping flown in turbulent conditions can affect

mapping coverage on non stabilized systems

• Aircraft is continuously pitching, yawing, rolling

• Roll directly affects LiDAR swath width

• Users must use Increased overlap or risk of coverage gaps

The Engineering Challenge

• Stabilize the sensor, not the aircraft

•

•

•

Fit within existing STC weight and volume limits

Fast roll response to gusts and turbulence

Integrate cleanly with existing modular pod design

• Maintain sensor flexibility and interchangeability

AISPECO’s Pod-Integrated Gimbal

Active roll stabilization inside an external wing pod

• Pod-integrated roll stabilization system

• High-torque brushless motor and fast ESC

• Closed-loop control using Applanix INS

systems

•

•

100 Hz roll feedback control loop

±15° active roll compensation

Validating the Concept
Real-world block mapping
mission

• Tested on real customer block mission

• Direct comparison: gimbaled vs non-gimbaled

•

•

Identical aircraft and flight conditions for both trajectories

1,000 ft AGL, 75° FOV, 30% overlap

Separating Aircraft
Motion from Sensor
Motion
Using dual IMUs for direct comparison

• Remote IMU mounted to LiDAR sensor

• Onboard IMU correlated directly to airframe (simulating non-

gimbaled roll)

• Two independently processed trajectories

• Synchronized using GPS time

Data Processing and Alignment

• Orientation bias removed between IMUs

• Static alignment used for calibration (stationary aircraft and

gimbal)

• Turns and non-mapping segments excluded

• Data segmented by individual flight lines

Results and Visualization

• Aircraft roll frequently exceeds data

gap threshold (±13.0 deg)

• Gimbal maintains near-nadir

orientation during flight line collection

• Significant reduction in gap-risk

events

• Consistent and centered swath

coverage

Operational Impact at
the Mission Scale
Fewer gaps, less overlap, fewer reflights

• Non-gimbaled: 8.6% of lines with gaps
• Gimbaled: 0.1% of lines with gaps
• ~180 seconds gap risk vs ~1 second
• Reduced need for greater overlap or re-flights with

gimbaled system

Conclusion

• Wing pods lowered cost and increased access

• Stability emerged as a limiting factor

• Gimbaled pod closes the performance gap

• Sensor stabilization improves efficiency and reliability
summary: The study focuses on overcoming stability challenges in aerial LiDAR mapping using wing pods. It demonstrates that integrating a gimballed system into an external wing pod significantly reduces gaps in LiDAR coverage compared to non-stabilized systems, improving overall efficiency and reliability.

Gaussian Splatting: Reconstructing the Real World

Radiance Fields bring the real world to a digital environment.

3DGS is like a photo, but from any angle.
Radiance Fields extend photography into lifelike and
interactive 3D, capturing depth, light, and perspective.

Reality, Rendered Naturally
By modeling view-dependent effects, Radiance Fields make
digital scenes appear as they do in real life.

From Visualization to Intelligence
Radiance Fields unlock automation and simulation, building
towards advanced construction insights.

Helios Visions

Radiance Fields Have Low Barrier to Entry

Radiance Fields are accessible, efficient, and cost-effective, making them ideal for widespread adoption.

Gaussian Splatting is created using standard 2D
images from any device.

Evolving Hardware Design
XGRIDS

Scalable compute for Enterprise applications.

Revisit Photogrammetry Data

Can coexist alongside Photogrammetry.

Michael Olsen, Ezra Che, Caleb Ogbeta

Olsen and Che have financial interests in the company

outcomes, or reporting of this research could benefit
EzDataMD LLC and could potentially benefit us.

2

Key Challenges

• Inherent measurement errors exist in the
context of ADA assessment on curb ramps
(device & methods)

• Unknown consistency in operation with many

entities

• ODOT has a rigorous process and training for

inspectors.

• Use of a single metric (Max) not considering a

flatness index for surface variability

• Unknown field effects over time
• QC measurements generally do not consider

concrete curing processes.

• Most industry tolerances do not directly apply to
curb ramps or rigorously consider measurement
errors.

• Many ramps have been deemed “noncompliant”

and require reconstruction.

3

ADA Standards for Curb Ramp Compliance
1:12 Max. Running Slope (8.3%)
• Design Max. 7.5% (ODOT)

1:48 Max. Cross Slope (2.1%)
• Design Max. 1.5% (ODOT)

Because a tolerance is not allowed, many agencies under design to a lower slope.

4

Examples

• City of Bellevue, WA, 2009

• 3511 Curb Ramps
• ADA Compliant Rate:
• Running Slope: 43%
• Cross Slope:
• Counter Slope: 67%
• Transition: 80%

31%

14% passed both!!!

57.7%

https://www.access-board.gov/guidelines-and-standards/buildings-and-sites/about-the-ada-standards/guide-to-the-ada-standards/chapter-4-ramps-and-curb-ramps

5

City of Portland Costs

• 35,000 residents with mobility disabilities

• $400 million US (Current Estimate)

• $113 million US (Initial Estimate)

• 18,000 ramps to be replaced

• 38,000 corners without ramps

• 1,500 ramps per year since 2018

• Legal settlement 7 years ago

• Concludes in 2030

“The total projected outlay for the
legally mandated program is likely
significantly higher because
Portland transportation officials
were unable to provide a full
accounting of program expenditures
to date.”

• Transportation maintenance backlog has soared to more than $6 billion.

• Miles of city neighborhoods lack sidewalks of any kind or even paved roads.

• City poised to eliminate more than 100 jobs and cut back on pothole repairs, street

paving, safety improvements and other basic services

Source: Kavanough, The Oregonian. 4/16/2025.
https://www.oregonlive.com/politics/2025/04/as-portland-sidewalk-curb-costs-surge-city-
weighs-massive-debt-financing-measure.html
6

Flatter is not really the right solution

7

Project objectives

Enable ODOT to reliably and systematically evaluate the methods and tools used
in the inspection process to achieve successful ADA compliance by:

• Investigating lidar technologies for ADA compliance assessments to evaluate

the performance of smart levels.

• Developing a research database of existing and newly-constructed curb ramps

for testing and analysis

• Identifying the best combination(s) of tools and methods

• Identifying an appropriate overall flatness index

• Establishing the expected variance for (1) instruments used to measure the
ramps, (2) flatness of the concrete material itself, and (3) movement or
settlement of a ramp to determine an industry tolerance for concrete sloped
planar surfaces considering the cumulative potential effects from the
aforementioned sources.

Key Takeaways

• Smart levels are simple but error prone.

• Calibration is critical. Obtain direct and reverse measurements.
Improved sampling strategies can reduce standard deviations.
•
• Vertical misalignments (e.g., debris or bump) affect slope readings

compared with horizontal misalignments.

• Max is hard to replicate.

 Lidar technology provides many benefits

 Systematic and repeatable
 0.2% standard deviation
 Usable for other measurements
 Georeferenced-reduces mix-up
Provides detailed digital record

Underground Mapping Using Remote Sensing Technology

A. John Lloyd, P.L.S.
DGT Associates
jlloyd@dgtassociates.com
508 769 2427

Today’s Topics

Mapping above-
and below-ground
environments

Advancing next-
generation remote
sensing R&D at DGT

Enhancing the
accuracy and reliability
of geospatial data

In New England,
professional land surveyors
have a long history of
mapping what lies
underground.

Massachusetts Transit Authority, 1894

“The records of most of the underground
pipes, sewers, and other structures laid
years ago were very imperfectly kept; in
fact, many such structures were built
without any records of their location
being made.”

June 1976 | Culver City, CA Tragedy.
The blast that changed the industry.

F A I L U R E   T O   M A P   E X I S T I N G   S I T E
C O N D I T I O N S

T H E   E R R O R

•

•

The National Transportation Safety Board
(NTSB) cited inadequacies in planning, design,
and construction practices as the cause of
the accident.

18-inch utility location error

C O N S E Q U E N C E

Nine people were killed, and 14 others were
severely injured.

Webster B. Todd Jr., NTSB:
“Someone should make damn certain exactly where that
pipeline is located. You don’t fool around in a situation
like that.”

How to Build a Highly Accurate Digital Utility Map
We will discuss how we capture and map the underground built environment.

Subsurface Information Modeling
(SIM): Building a Digital Twin

Is the data authentic or aesthetic?

Creating a comprehensive 3D model that
integrates above- and below-ground elements
presents a significant challenge.

T H E   S O L U T I O N
Subsurface Utility Mapping.  Defining a Standard of Care.

The ASCE Subsurface Utility Mapping standard
defines four data quality classifications.

A S C E   3 8 - 2 2   Q U A L I T Y   L E V E L   D   D A T A

Primarily used in the planning stage of A project

ASCE Quality Level D data is compiled
through a desktop survey.

• Gather all available records from public and private owners to compile a plan

showing all known facilities.

• No field investigation is required.

• Cooperation between facility and asset owners is vital to building a successful

SUM plan using Quality Level D data.

A S C E   3 8 - 2 2   Q U A L I T Y   L E V E L   C   D A T A

Used in the Preliminary Design Stage of a project

• The survey locates visible surface utility features and correlates available

information about underground asset owners, enabling informed decision-making.

• No below-ground investigation involved in Quality Level C data.

ASCE Quality Level C data is collected
through an on-site survey.

The ASCE Subsurface Utility Mapping standard
defines four data quality classifications.

• Traditional methods for road condition assessment and safety analysis, such as field surveying, are not safe, efficient, or cost-effective.

used by DOTs for roadway data collection, including

• Tight coupling of IMU and LiDAR
• Correspondences from multiple LiDAR scan directions (NFB scanning)

• Evaluation of consistency/precision and accuracy

STRIPmap Acquisition is purpose-built for the precise mapping of corridor features such as
utility rights-of-way, pipelines, roads, and railways. When operated in STRIP mode, the
sensor collects data at a resolution of 20,000 pixels along the track and 14,000 pixels across
the track. This method significantly reduces the number of images and seamlines, enhancing
the usability of image pixels within the defined corridor area of interest (AOI). Its
effectiveness is further enhanced when integrated with LiDAR data.

This larger usable footprint

• Enhances triangulation
• Assists with color balancing
• Reduces control/tie point requirements
• Facilitates seamline QC/editing for mosaics
• Cuts down on both images and processing time by 50%

AI Intelligence Layer

LLMs (Large Language Models)

Physical AI (Presentation Focus)

Work with text and symbols

Work with sensors, images, spatial data, and machines

• Domain: Text, code, documents.
• Mechanism: Probabilistic prediction.
• Role: The Assistant.
• Limitation: Hallucinates; blindly unaware of physical laws

like gravity.

Mainstream Example : ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude

LLMs are brilliant at processing text. But they do not understand

rock mechanics or gravity. Using them for calculation was a

mistake.

• Domain: images, point clouds, videos, drill data, sensors.
• Good at: seeing, measuring, predicting outcomes.
• Mechanism: Computer Vision, Physics Engines.
• Output: numbers tied to the site.
• Limitation: bad calibration and weak feedback loops.
Mainstream Example : GPT -4V, NVIDIA Cosmos, World
Models

Simulation-Based Training: Digital Twins for training and testing
complex physical interactions (rigid body dynamics, lighting).

physical laws, not just data patterns ~ Blast Movement

Multimodal Integration: Combining sensory data (vision, touch)

Term

Digital Twin

Photogrammetry

LiDAR (Light Detection And Ranging)

Unmanned Aerial System (UAS)

AI (Artificial Intelligence)

Machine Learning

Machine Vision

Definition

A digital twin is a dynamic virtual representation of a physical object, process,
system, or environment that is continuously updated with real-time data from its
real-world counterpart

The science of making measurements and creating models from photographs,
which allows for the extraction of 3D information from 2D images.

A remote sensing technology that uses pulsed laser light to measure distances and
create 3D maps of an environment

An unmanned aircraft and all the necessary equipment required for its safe and
efficient operation, including the aircraft itself and its ground control systems

The ability of computer systems to perform tasks that typically require human
intelligence, such as learning, problem-solving, and decision-making

The process of using algorithms to enable computers to learn from data, identify
patterns, and make predictions or decisions without being explicitly programmed

A set of rules that allows different software applications to communicate with
each other

Uses cameras and software to enable machines to "see," interpret, and make
decisions from visual data often utilizing AI/deep learning for complex analysis

Existing Methods and Limitations

Robotic Total Station

Inertial “Stitching”

• Provides Precise Horizontal and

Vertical Position

• Requires Constant Line-of-Sight
• No Pitch, Roll, or Heading (Yaw)
• Low-precision Time Synchronization

• Provides Pitch, Roll, and Heading (Yaw)
• Does not require Line-of-Sight
• Provides Time Synchronization
• Subject to IMU “drift”; Accuracy

decreases with time; Requires limited
turns and short outage durations

Simultaneous
Localization and
Mapping (SLAM)
• Commonly used for indoor
mapping, i.e. BIM surveys

•

Intensive cloud-to-cloud feature
extraction and matching algorithm

• No GNSS required

• End result is a cohesive point

cloud using relative coordinates

Can SLAM technology be applied to
hydrographic surveys?

SLAM Limitations for
Hydrographic Surveys

• Seafloor may not contain sufficient

features/topography for cloud-to-cloud matching

• Acoustic beam width is significantly greater than
LiDAR resulting in lower resolution and precision

• Typical multibeam sensors are configured as a swath
(similar to Aerial LiDAR); SLAM sensors are configured
with a 360 view to continuously “see” the surroundings

• Limited to point cloud surveys (i.e. Multibeam); could
not be used for Singlebeam, Sub-Bottom, Side Scan,
or Magnetometer surveys.

Solution:  Use SLAM indirectly to
determine Vessel Position & Orientation

Navigation

Vessel

Offsets measured between LiDAR sensor
and hydrographic survey sensors

Extract vessel position and orientation
from SLAM algorithm (relative coordinates)

Format as “SLAMbet” (Time, Lat, Lon,
Height, Pitch, Roll, Yaw)

Apply “SLAMbet” to hydrographic data

No GNSS required

High Precision Horizontal and
Vertical Position; Pitch, Roll, and
Yaw

Does not require Line-of-Sight

Not subject to IMU drift

Parameters?

PRESENTS

GEOFRAME

Interior Camera Calibration

Exterior Camera Localization

Emlid Flow Photo Capture on
Reach RS4 Pro

What Worked?

High Ground Resolution

The project achieved an average Ground Sampling Distance (GSD) of
2.9 cm, providing high-detail surface data.

Substantial Data Generation

Despite calibration issues, the pipeline successfully generated a dense
point cloud containing 2,421,760 points.

Effective Area Coverage

The processing covered a significant area of 25.758 hectares.

Efficient Point Cloud Processing

While the initial calibration took nearly 3 hours, the dense point cloud
generation was completed in just 11 minutes and 44 seconds using
hardware acceleration.

Camera Optimization

For the images that did calibrate, the relative difference between initial
and optimized internal camera parameters was very low (0.68%),
indicating stable sensor modeling for those units.

What didn’t work

Poor Image Calibration

A major failure point was that only 24.28% of images (926 out of 3814)
were successfully calibrated.

Insufficient Keypoint Matches

The median number of matches per image was 252, which is flagged as
"Low" (the threshold for a healthy project is typically >1000).

Fragmentation

The dataset split into 35 separate calibration blocks, meaning the
software could not stitch the entire drive into a single, continuous
model.

High Geolocation Variance

There were significant absolute geolocation errors, particularly in the X-
axis, which showed an RMS error of 1.800 meters.

Weak Inter-Camera Links

Many camera positions show "near-transparent" links in the 2D
matches plot, indicating weak connectivity that requires manual tie
points or better overlap to fix.

Special thanks to Dr. Charles Toth, our
Sponsors, and The Survey School Community
summary: The study evaluated the use of Tesla Sensor Suite for mobile mapping, achieving high ground resolution but facing challenges such as poor image calibration and insufficient keypoint matches.

2025-2035 Geospatial Workforce Trends:

1. Geospatial sector is growing faster than the
workforce can support.
2. Demand for advanced skills continues to surge.
3. Graduate output and early-career pipelines lag far
behind demand.
4. Rapid technology shifts and cross-industry
competition are widening the talent gap and raising
the urgency for coordinated action.

•

manual (only) production to AI
• Government hiring remains persistent
• Higher education is evolving,  community college programs are growing,
university are becoming partners in addressing the geo workforce gap

• Project goals:

• Challenges

History of the Yakima–
Tieton Irrigation Canal

• Early 1900s Origins

Built under the U.S. Bureau of Reclamation’s Yakima Project
to bring reliable water to central Washington’s arid farmland.

• Part of the Yakima River Basin Development

Established to support agricultural expansion in one of the
West’s most promising but water-limited regions.

• Engineering & Construction

Concrete-lined canal designed to convey water from the
Tieton River across miles of varied terrain; later modernized
to improve flow efficiency and reduce seepage.

• Agricultural Impact

Enabled the region’s growth into a major producer of apples,
hops, grapes, and diverse crops by providing consistent
irrigation.

• Current Importance

Aging infrastructure now requires advanced documentation
and assessment—making it an ideal case study for modern
hybrid surveying workflows.

Establishing Control

• Leica total station + GNSS receivers

• TS16 and GS18

• Control network layout

• Over 175 points set over corridor
• Some were difficult to identify under vegetation
• RMSE 0.095’ over whole site

• Capturing canal flow line
• Ensuring consistent accuracy across datasets

Aerial LiDAR
Acquisition

• Inspired Flight IF800 platform
• GeoCue TrueView 540 sensor
• Capturing surrounding ground & vegetation
• Benefits for long linear corridors

Terrestrial Laser Scanning

• Leica RTC360
• Scan coverage inside canal
• Strengths
• Detail
• Geometry
• Condition documentation

• Challenges

• Control Targets
• Cloud to Cloud Registration

Bringing the Data Together

scans with

line, surface
model, and
canal interior

and Reporting

Software used:
• LP360
• Register 360
• 3DR
• Leica Infinity

Lessons Learned

• Importance of good control
• Managing multiple data types
• Training and workflow consistency
• Data management considerations

Key Takeaways

• Traditional + modern tech = best results
• Hybrid workflows reduce field time and risk
• Scalable approach for many corridor projects

Conclusions

Lessons from the trenches

● Standards remove vendor lock-in and let clients interoperate.
● Use 3D Tiles / glTF as for 3D  and OGC APIs for raster/vector 2D serving. Check also COG and STAC.

● Automate conversion and provenance

● Build repeatable pipelines.
● Produce provenance metadata and previews so outputs are auditable and retryable

● Design & implement for real users

● Deliver filtered, styled, LOD-aware views, measurement and annotation.
● Client-side styling + attribute overlays beats republishing tiles for every view

● Plan operations & data governance early

● Implement CDN + cache invalidation, tile versioning, monitoring and rollback procedures.
● Decide where open data applies and formalize access/governance for restricted datasets.
Standards, Automation, UX, Operations & governance, the
ingredients that make urban twins operational

Future works

● Support of Obj files to 3D Tiles conversion pipeline
● Support of IFC and CityGML files to 3D Tiles conversion pipeline
As far as MapStore more work is ongoing:
● Improved filtering for 3D Tiles
● Support for OGC GeoVolumes
● Better overlay support for OGC Services in 3D
● Integration with sensor data & simulation models!

•

Advanced geospatial data
analytics

processing

•

2D/3D visualization/spatial
modeling for environmental &
infrastructure applications

Project Introduction

• sUAS + RGB + LiDAR +
Thermal in extreme
conditions

• Steep terrain, no cell

coverage, fire impacts,
toxic dusts

• Safe, defensible data
without direct access

• Case study framework for
remote, high-risk sites

Site Context & Objectives

• Decommissioned mercury

mine; toxic dusts (heavy metals,
asbestos); rugged, unstable
terrain

• Extremely remote; no cell

coverage; limited safe access

• Project Objectives:

• Build high-res basemaps
• Create 3D building models
• Reconnaissance for potential
mine openings/to confirm
adequate closure of workings

Constraints & Safety Approach

• Precipitous slopes, unstable ground,
toxic dusts, 4WD roads, night ops (to
limit thermal interference)

• FAA-certified pilots; trained visual

observers

• Risk assessment & dynamic JSA
• Redundant comms (radios + satellite;

local EMS notified), PPE, and
equipment

• SOPs: night ops, crew rest cycles,

lost-link, crew VO placement, hazard
buffers

• Camp logistics: food safety, staging,

lighting, first aid, evacuation

Platforms & Payloads

• DJI Matrice 350 RTK Drone

(endurance, stability, flexibility)

• Zenmuse P1 (RGB) for

photogrammetry

• Zenmuse L1 (LiDAR) for precise point

clouds

• Zenmuse H20T (thermal + zoom) for

night anomalies & inspection
missions

• Phantom 4 Pro V2 for panoramas
• Robodot Base Station & Touch High-
accuracy GNSS Receivers (RTK to
PPK)

• Day + night capability matched to

objectives

Platform & Payload images courtesy of  DJI.com

Mission Planning & Coverage

Flight Plans Tailored to Deliverables:
• Autonomous missions for

orthomosaic (target ~0.3 in/px)

• Grid + Oblique flights for

facades/rooflines (3D models)

• Night thermal corridors over potential

mine openings/drainage
pathways/previous workings
• VO positions to maintain LOS in

complex terrain

• GCPs/checkpoints where safe (LiDAR
area only due to access constraints)

• Terrain-aware flight planning with

offline DEMs

Flight Operations & Data Capture

•
•
•
•
•

• Sequenced day/night priorities by weather & light
9 flight home bases (i.e., set-up points)
5 overnight missions
7 daytime missions
17 panoramas collected
4 areas across site with high-resolution
orthomosaic basemaps & thermal
1 area covered with LiDAR

•

• Battery logistics: pre-warm, rotation, camp

charging

•

>250 GB raw data captured

•

235,000,000 points in the LiDAR point cloud
of Camp 1 with 11 GCPs used

• Nightly integrity checks & redundant backups via

SSDs & Starlink upload to the Cloud

• Quick-look QA to confirm coverage after each

mission; minimize re-flies

Processing Workflow

• RGB: align → calibrate → GCPs

→ orthomosaic → QA/QC
(DroneDeploy)

• LiDAR: trajectory → GCPs →

colorize → classify (TerraScan /
Global Mapper)

• Thermal: radiometric calibration
→ align w/RGB → holistically
review (Esri GIS Stack)

• Common reference frames;

cross-product checks (Esri GIS
Stack)

Basemaps & 3D Models

• Orthomosaic,

DSM/DTM, textured 3D
mesh (~0.3 in/px)

• Total RMSE in XYZ is

0.078’ or 0.94”
• Access planning with
measurable detail
• Erosion/drainage

interpretation

• Structural context in

accurate terrain

High-Resolution Zoom Inspections

LiDAR for Structural Assessment

• Vegetation penetration
reveals ground surface
• Dimensions, clearances,
structural component
quantification

Collapsed Roof
Materials

• Slices/profiles support
engineering judgment

• Defensible

measurements where
access is limited

Thermal Night Missions & Findings
• Night ops to detect potential
airflow/drainage anomalies
• Low, slow passes to boost

signal quality

• Sought out potential hotspots

along drainage; anomalies near
workings

• Validation with RGB + terrain to

reduce false positives

• Findings confirm workings have

been sealed adequately

Thermal Drone Inspection Example

Delivery & Client Interaction

• Interactive DroneDeploy

portal for intuitive
exploration

• ArcGIS integration:

dashboards, layer stacks,
point clouds, maps

• Stakeholder comments &

prioritization

• Faster cycle from capture

to decisions

Outcomes, Lessons & Recommendations

Documented inaccessible areas comprehensively

Confirmed potential mine openings have been sealed

LiDAR-backed structural insights

Lessons: comms/equipment redundancy; SOPs for night/thermal; weather during
crew rest cycles

Recommendations: pair thermal with terrain; standardize VO positions; backup
equipment/processing/storage; in-field QA/QC checks

Key Takeaways:

Tech-Savvy Exploration: From RGB to LiDAR and thermal sensors, our drones
weren’t just flying—they were transforming remote mine inspection into a
high-tech adventure.

Mission Possible: Tackling steep terrains, zero cell reception, and intense
safety protocols, we camped out and conquered every challenge to make the
drone missions a soaring success.

Data Overload: With over 250GB of raw data, we crafted stunning high-res
basemaps, 3D models, and super-detailed images, all showcased on
interactive platforms for an up-close and personal look!

Thank you!

Sarah Ostertag

Senior Lead Consultant, Geospatial Analysis | UAV Pilot

Galago by Ramboll

sostertag@ramboll.com

Scan to connect with me on LinkedIn:

Bathymetric LiDAR provides a geometric measurement.
Emits a known pulse of green laser light (typically 532 nm).
Measures time-of-flight with a correction for the refractive index of water.
Accuracy is driven by timing precision, not water color.

Hyperspectral sensors record a radiometric estimation based on spectral reflectance
of the water column and bottom.  Depth must be derived from, water absorption,
scattering, bottom albedo, sun angle, surface glint, turbidity, atmospheric path
radiance.

• Works day or night

• Works in low sun
• Works in moderate sea state

Shallow water bathymetry is achievable with hyperspectral imaging, but it provides a derived estimate
and requires specific environmental conditions for accuracy.

Bathymetric LiDAR delivers direct, geometric depth measurements, while hyperspectral sensors offer
detailed information on submerged aquatic vegetation (SAV) and water column properties.

Combining hyperspectral imaging with LiDAR data enhances understanding of underwater terrain, SAV
health, and species composition, supporting ecosystem monitoring and management.

The fusion of these technologies provides valuable insights for fisheries, restoration, planning, policy,
regulation, and scientific research.

and Processing

Challenges

• Weather

•

Vegetation (Lidar)

Topo-bathymetric Lidar Acquisition

• Leica HawkEye-5 sensor suite
• Sensor is flown at 400-500m altitude
• Each line is up to 50km long
• Typical operations @480m altitude and 125kts:

• Planned for lidar swath width of ~350m, with 20% overlap of lines

• Can reach water depths of up to 50m (clear water)

Challenges - Turbidity

Example from the Florida Everglades

•

Initial data coverage from collection indicated
minimal bathymetric returns due to water conditions.
• No expectation that there would be viable bathymetry

• Post-processing using automation allowed for

reclassification of returns as bathymetry that were
initially classified as noise.

in such a turbid environment.

• Data QC’d to ensure automated processes correctly

classified bathymetry.

CH4: Sable Island Topo-Photo Mission

• 15-line mission to fully cover the island topography.
• Lines planned at 800 m AGL to minimize time on-site.

• Note that planning imagery from 2019 when the NE

section was above water.

Target point position RMSE <0.01 m
Final laser mission RMSE <0.05 m (Topo)

CH4: Bathy at 800 m
• Typical bathymetric data collection is limited to 400-500 m for the CH4X platform.
• It was evident that bathy was being collected at 800 m (blue-green points). The extent and

quality of the bathymetric data was surprising.

CH4: NIR-Green Laser vegetation penetration
• Sable Island is covered in thick marram grasses.
• It was expected that these grasses would be impossible to penetrate and

extra efforts would be needed to estimate elevations beneath and separate
thick and flat vegetation from ground points.

Wide footprint Green laser reflected ground through dense marram
• Only the bathy laser was able to penetrate the dense marram grass.
• The 4-band image NDVI refined vegetation classification and provided increased

confidence in laser point ground classification.

• Ground/vegetation classification would have been challenging without implementing a

multi sensor approach.

• Bathymetric returns from green laser at 800 m AGL

Hydrographic Applications of TB-lidar

• Excluding nautical chart updating
• Benthic Habitat Mapping (various SAV) – Plug for Advanced Remote Sensing Data Classification

Approaches - 2/18/2025 from 11:30:00 AM - 11:45:00 AM in Room Bluebird 3E

• Geologic & Geomorphic Mapping – nearshore currents remove sediment
• Coastal Hazard Mapping – HD storm surge models, erosion, waves…
• Modelling the Fate of Nearshore Pollution – HD modelling
• Improved River Flood Hazard Mapping – HD modelling with river channels
• River Fish Habitat and Fish Passage Mapping & modelling drought and
flood conditions to support River Restoration efforts for Improved Fish
Habitat & Passage
• Mapping Shipwrecks
• Port Development-resource extraction in the north, Defence – near-shore

landing sites, camp deployment….

Conclusions

• The combination of NIR (narrow footprint) and GREEN (wide

footprint) lasers provide complimentary information

• For topographic sites, the green laser can supplement the NIR for
ground detection under the canopy (e.g. forest, marram grass)
• Green laser picks up narrow targets – utility lines…clothesline
• Even at 800m green laser provided valid bathy returns Sable Is.
• Multiple applications of seamless topo-bathy DEMs

Advantages and Challenges of Mapping
Riverine Environments with Aerial
Topobathymetric Lidar

Applications of Remotely Sensed Topobathy Data II

Jackson McGraw, Senior Geospatial Analyst

Geo Week 2026

February 17, 2026

11

Mapping Riverine Environments Using Aerial
Topobathymetric Lidar

Imagery courtesy of: USGS

What Does this Mean?
• The use of aerial lidar to acquire elevation
data of riverbeds and surrounding terrain

• Produce seamless mapping products

combining topography with bathymetry

Shenandoah River

• Dewberry has delivered on a variety of

riverine topobathy projects…

22

Diagram courtesy of: NOAA NGS

Williamson River

• Approximately 504 square

miles

• Included portions of the

Upper Klamath Lake and the
Klamath Marsh Wildlife
Refuge

• Client

• Fish & Wildlife Service

• Sensors

• CZMIL SuperNova

• RIEGL VQ-880-GII

33

Upper Mississippi River Restoration

• Approximately 35 square miles

• Two sections of the Mississippi
River along the Minnesota-
Wisconsin border

• Final products integrated with

sonar data

44

• Client

• US Army Corps of Engineers

• Sensor

• CZMIL SuperNova

Virginia Riverine
• Approximately 312 sq mi, across multiple

river systems

• Appomattox River

• Dan River

•

James River

• North Fork Shenandoah River

• Rappahannock River

• Roanoke River

• Shenandoah River

• South Fork Shenandoah River

• South River

• Client

• NOAA NGS

• Sensor

• CZMIL SuperNova

55

Gulf Coast: Florida

Topobathy Lidar Mapping

• Not just rivers

• Aerial

topobathymetric
lidar is used to
map a variety of
other saltwater
and freshwater
environments

66

Other Common Environments that Leverage
Topobathy Lidar Mapping

Lakes

77

Lake Michigan

Other Common Environments that Leverage
Topobathy Lidar Mapping
Miscellaneous Aquatic
Environments:
Wetlands, Ponds, Springs, Lagoons, etc.

88

Fish Springs NWR, UT

Other Common Environments that Leverage
Topobathy Lidar Mapping
Coastlines

99

Gulf Coast, FL

Coastal vs. Riverine Topobathy Lidar Mapping

• Historically, coastal shoreline mapping has been a greater focus of this technology

compared to riverine environments

• Economical

• National Shoreline

• Maritime Commercial Shipping

• Natural Disaster Planning and Recovery

• Erosion Mitigation & Flood Modeling

• Hurricane Recovery

• Technological

• Sensor capabilities

• Emerging growth in riverine topobathy mapping with technological advances

• Presents both challenges and advantages

1010

Image courtesy of: NOAA

Challenges of Mapping Riverine Environments
with Topobathy Lidar

River Flight Plan

Coastal Flight Plan

• Flight Planning

• Flooding and runoff are more compounded in a river systems

• Smaller/narrower water features, additional influx from tributaries

• More constrained flying opportunities

• Leaf-off, no ice

• Winding corridor planning, while necessary, yields less efficient acquisition

River Survey Point

Coastal Survey Point

• Cost per sq mi typically higher for rivers

• More time in turn, more lines per mission

• Example: per 2 sq mi, coast = 10 flightlines, river = 36 flightlines

• Survey

• Rivers typically have fewer accessible survey locations

• These locations can be more challenging to survey as rivers are typically less developed over long stretches

• Benthic composition can also be more hazardous than coastlines (river rocks vs sandy beaches)

1111

Challenges of Mapping Riverine Environments
with Topobathy Lidar

Veg Overhand and Turbidity
Withrow Creek, NC

• Acquisition

• Rivers often have more challenging terrain to fly over

• Think of a narrow river valley compared to flat coastal AOI

• Can’t always fly safely at optimal height above ground level

• Veg overhang typically a greater hurdle, especially narrow rivers

• Turbidity can be a greater challenge

• Runoff sediment funneling into a river has less water volume

to disperse into than in coastal environments

• Close monitoring of river flow rates and water levels needed

• Dams and locks

1212

Large Dam on the James River, VA

Riverine Acquisition in
Challenging Terrain

Challenges of Mapping Riverine Environments
with Topobathy Lidar

Multiple Riverbanks

• Data Processing, Analysis,

and Classification
• Increased Relative Shoreline

• Rivers always have two or more banks

• Basic coastline only has one

• Exceptions: Nearshore islands,

peninsulas, bays, complex
shorelines, etc.

• On a relative basis, rivers tend to have more

shoreline than coastal environments

• More time and resources for the processing,

analysis, and classification

1313

Single Shoreline

Challenges of Mapping Riverine Environments
with Topobathy Lidar
• Data Processing, Analysis, and

Steep River Gradient

Classification:
• Water Surface Variability

• Rivers typically have drastically more variability in water levels

than coastlines

• Exceptions: slow-moving rivers like the Miss. River

Lidar Pulses and Returns
Refracting in Water

• Can have major impacts on refraction corrections

• Refraction is depth dependent

• More water surface changes = more refraction variability

and increased refraction correction efforts

• Increased gradient also increases likelihood of rapids and

whitewater conditions which can obscure bathymetric returns

1414

Advantages of Mapping Riverine Environments
with Topobathy Lidar
• Bathy Bottom Composition

• Rivers tend to have rockier, less sandy bathy bottom compositions

• Typically, less dynamic than coastal bathy bottom compositions

• Harder composition can yield cleaner bathy bottom returns

1515

Image courtesy of: USGS

Image courtesy of: NPS

Advantages of Mapping Riverine Environments
with Topobathy Lidar

Gulf Coast, FL

• Temporal

• Riverbeds tend to have significantly fewer temporal fluctuations

compared to coastlines during acquisition periods

• Sandy seafloors and coastal shorelines are highly dynamic

• Tides, currents, wave action

• We’ve seen significant temporal offsets in coastal bathy

bottom surfaces mere days apart

Red = 3/01/2024  |  Green = 3/15/2024 (Bathy bottoms +30cm DZ)

Coastal missions showing temporal offsets in bathy surfaces were collected 2 weeks part

1616

Advantages of Mapping Riverine Environments
with Topobathy Lidar

• Temporal
• Everything

changes over time,
rivers are no
exception

• Temporal offsets in

riverbeds are
typically less of a
concern within
normal acquisition
timeframes

• Image: Flooding in a new mission caused impenetrable turbidity in the Dan River (VA) during the first acquisition

attempt

• Reflights did not show temporal offsets in the bathymetry

1717

Advantages of Mapping Riverine Environments
with Topobathy Lidar

Dan River: Flooded

• Tidal Influence and Wave Action

• No tidal influences

• Besides estuarine tidal reaches

• Tidal influences increase likelihood of temporal

shifting in the bathy bottom

• Wave Action

• Less pronounced in rivers

• Tend not to obscure valid bathy bottom returns

compared to coastlines

Dan River: Subsided Flooding and Turbidity

• Water Gauges/Monitoring Stations

• Rivers typically have more water gauges on a

relative basis than coastlines

• River gauges often provide more data than coastal
monitoring stations, which are crucial for assessing
flow rates and water levels

1818

Image courtesy of: USGS

Closing Points

• Less high-detailed bathymetric data exists for rivers compared to coastlines

• Many challenges, but aerial topobathymetric lidar technologies and strategies are advancing

• Greater opportunities to map our rivers and better understand how they influence inland:

• Watershed Management

• Hydrological Modeling

• Flood Risk Assessments

• Environmental Monitoring

• Resource Management

• Highly turbid, shallow rivers will be a challenge for

the foreseeable future

• For the acquisition of high-detailed elevation data
of riverbeds and their surrounding topographies,
aerial topobathymetric lidar technology is typically

• Fastest

• Most agile

• Cheapest

1919

Thank you!

Questions?

2020

Visit us at Booth #1511

From the Air and Under the Water

Integrating UAS Lidar and Traditional Bathymetric
Data for Reservoir Management

Geo Week 2026
February 16-18, 2026 | Colorado Convention Center | Denver, CO, USA

Introduction

Comparing airborne lidar with bathymetric
data: how use LP360 for merging

Integrated drone lidar data collection
(topography) with traditional echo sounder
surveys (bathymetry).

Used LP360 software suite for:

• TrueView lidar sensor processing.
• Geometric adjustment/correction and accuracy

verification.
•
Import of bathymetric datasets.
•
Integrated QA/QC of merged point clouds.
• Generation of integrated surface models.
• Volumetric analysis as input to Area-Volume-

Elevation (AVE) curves.

Rio Grande do Sul/Canastra

Rio Grande do Sul/Canastra

• Performed in conjunction with Companhia

Siderúrgica Nacional (CSN) (Brazilian
National Steel Company)

• Terra Brasil surveyed multiple dam sites for

CSN in January 2024 using a TV515.

• Bathymetry collected over extended period.

• The study region experienced catastrophic

flooding in April/May 2024.

• As a result of repair work due to the flooding,
the opportunity to re-survey the Canastra dam
site “dry” was available in August 2024.

• Full topography was re-surveyed using TV540

with the reservoir empty.

DTM

Equipment Deployed

• All data was collected using three main sensors:

• TrueView 515 (905 nm)

• TrueView 540 (1.5 μm)

• South SDE-28s (Digital Single Beam, 14-30 Hz Ping Rate, Echo Sounder )

• Drone flights typically at 70 m AGL, 5 m/s.

• Previously collected bathymetric data was also integrated, where available.

• GNSS base stations for PPK processing along with multiple Ground Check

Points (GCPs) for accuracy assessment and geometric correction.

• Overall accuracies in the 1-10 cm range.

Accuracy Analysis (Lidar)

• 22 GCPs compared to lidar

point cloud in LP360.

• Vertical checks done against

lidar TIN surface.

• Horizontal checks done

against known/identifiable
survey marks.

• RMSEz error of ~3.5 cm.

Brazil Floods

Reservoir “Dry” Topography

• Allowed for

topographic surface
to bathymetric
surface comparison
and volumetric
difference analysis
across entire basin.

• Able to ground truth
the prior bathymetry
data with allowance
for the temporal
difference in collects.

Bathymetry vs. “Dry” Topography

• Comparison of transects (cross sections) demonstrates fit between
echo sounder bathymetry and ground truth from topographic lidar.

Bathymetry
2 pts/m (along line) at 20 m line spacing.
3,034 bathymetric points.
Avg. < 0.1 ppsm

Topography

TV540 Sampling ~ 1,250 ppsm
(Flown with 50% overlap.)

TV515 + South SDE-28s

TV540

Deliverables

• Deliverables included standard
derived products from the point

• Seamless DTM (Raster)

Largest volume with
smallest surface area
for optimum cost-
benefit.

• Primary application-specific

analysis was an Area-Volume-

• Area-Volume at each Elevation (10

cm resolution).

• Used to optimize water storage,

mitigate flood risk, improve power
generation efficiency, and aid in
water supply planning.

Flooded vs Dry Reservoir

Pedra Reservoir

• Similar analysis was performed for

Electrobras (the major electric
utility), but with legacy-only
topographic data (no drone data
collected, only updated bathymetry).

•

Integrated existing topographic data
(contours) from Electrobras archive,
converted to point cloud in LP360.

• Terra Brasil performed bathymetry of
42 sq. km at a 30 m line spacing
resulting in 1,874,074 bathymetry
points.

Pedra Reservoir

Pedra Reservoir – Bathymetry Data in LP360

Effective display and surface modelling for any point
cloud data in LP360.

Elejor Hydroelectric Plant

• Fundao and Santa Clara

reservoirs, part of the Elejor
hydroelectric plants on the
Jordao River.

• Combined drone topographic
lidar (TV540) with traditional
bathymetry (South SDE-28s; 2
m posting at 40 m spaced
lines).

• Integrated point clouds in

LP360 to generate seamless
DTM model and
corresponding AVE curves.

Elejor Hydroelectric Plant

Additional Examples

Additional Examples

Surveyors Have Nice Offices …

Conclusions:

• Cost-effective method for
updating of AVE curves.

• Varying point densities from
different sources must be
integrated correctly when
generating seamless DTM.

• QA/QC and accuracy

checking of both datasets
must be consistent.

• Robust SRS support and

transformation/reprojection
tools are mandatory.

Thank you!

features

Post processing for this model is:

• Less straight forward

• Varying MMUs by class

3232

Geo Week: Applied Image Processing, Analysis, and Classification 2/17/2026

Automating Raster Processing

Raster is Faster is not always true...

• Tiling is your friend

• Tiling allows for better concurrent processing

• Beware edge artifacts/holes from mosaicking tiles

• Handling time is non-linear with data size

• GDAL provides great tools for raster data management

3333

Geo Week: Applied Image Processing, Analysis, and Classification 2/17/2026

Automating Raster Processing

When preprocessing data:

• Try to leverage compiled languages

• Vectorize functions when possible

• Avoid nesting loops excessively

• Avoid mosaicking in lieu of VRTs

• Handle it once! (ELT)

3434

Geo Week: Applied Image Processing, Analysis, and Classification 2/17/2026

Automating Raster Processing

Automation only makes sense when human review time is not
needed!

• This creates natural break points

• Use these to break points well:

• Allow for distributed processing across machines

• Subsetting datasets for constrained testing

•

Longer running processes run more risk of random errors
 Dropped bits, connection time outs, IT interference, etc.

3535

Geo Week: Applied Image Processing, Analysis, and Classification 2/17/2026

Automating Raster Processing

Minutia:

• File naming and folder management

• Data retention

• Spawning subprocesses

• Cores + Multithreading

• Getting around the GIL

• Modular processing

3636

Geo Week: Applied Image Processing, Analysis, and Classification 2/17/2026

Automating Raster Processing

Decide on what level of automation is useful!

• Models in development do not need a final E2E processing

pipeline

• Preprocessed data to try other algorithms

• Can your machine keep up with the automation

• High-throughput processing will quite literally wear out a CPU/GPU

3737

•

LASTools

• Old Workflow, with parallelism, ETA ~36 hours

• R-LIDAR (lasR)

• Arcpy (Spatial Analyst)

• White Box

• New HFE workflow: Python with relevant subprocess calls to R

and White Box

• New Workflow, with parallelism, ETA ~6 hours

The Damage Assessment of 2019–2020 Australia Bushfire to
the Endangered Ecological Communities in Eurobodalla
Shire Using Object-Based Classification

Dylan Anderson a., Jae Sung Kim a

a Department of Civil, Environmental, and Geospatial Engineering, Michigan Technological University,
Houghton, Michigan, USA, 49931

2/17/2026

GEO WEEK 2026, ASPRS, February 16-18, 2026, Denver, Colorado

1

Introduction

• Wildfires cause immense environmental and societal damage, making rapid assessment

critical and exponentially beneficial.

• Scientists, engineers, and emergency responders have the potential to benefit from the use

of remote sensing in wildfire damage assessment.

• Remote sensing + GIS tools (ArcGIS Pro) now allow fast, repeatable wildfire damage

mapping.

2

Introduction

• Australia has a history of catastrophic wildfire events, including the 2019-2020
“Black Summer Wildfires” with unprecedented intensity and magnitude. The
black fires caused a new level of fear, destruction, and displacement.

• The main catalysts of ignition and intensity of Black Summer Wildfires were
mainly (1) drought; (2) record-breaking high ambient air temperatures[1].

• Eurobodalla Shire, located in New South Wales, uses 31 % of the land for timber

production and 20 % for agriculture.

•

In the Eurobodalla Shire, Black Summer Wildfires damaged 150 million dollars of
the economy and affected more than 81% of the local government area.

3

Introduction

• New South Wales, Australia, defines “Ecological Community” as “an assemblage

of species occupying a particular area”[3].

•

It is crucial to find the damaged area of endangered ecological communities
(EECs) by the Black Summer Wildfires as the first step for a future EECs recovery
plan.

4

Research Objective

•

Identify the damaged area of EECs in Eurobodalla Shire, Australia by Black
Summer Wildfires.

• Provide the EECs’ damaged area estimation workflow using object-based image

analysis

5

Area of Analysis

•

•

•

Eurobodalla Shire has importance in tourism, conservation, timber production and agriculture [4].
Eurobodalla Shire is one of the most impacted areas by 2019-2020 Black Summer Wildfires[5].
Fire activities were verified using ESRI’s Sentinel-2 Landcover Explorer [6].

• We still lack the quantified study about the damage to Eurobodalla Shire by Black Summer Wildfires.

The percentage of damaged area of EECs

19

Conclusion

• High-resolution Sentinel-2 imagery was acquired using the Copernicus Browser, which is publicly

available web service, collecting false color composite bands.

• In segmentation step, a trial and error of multiple spatial, spectral, and minimum cell size inputs

were conducted until the correct balance was discovered to categorize the best segmented pixels

into reasonable classes.

• Large numbers of training sample objects were collected to refine the accuracy and to

differentiate between similar segmented objects.

• The SVM classification resulted a overall accuracies and Kappa percentage greater than 90

percent for both images.

20

Conclusion

• A comparison of polygon areas revealed an approximate forest land cover change of 64 %.

• In the final results, Lowland Grassy Woodland in the South East Corner Bioregion was found to

be severely damaged by 47.881

, which addresses 66.49 % of the original area.

• ArcGIS Pro classification wizard made whole object-based image classification easy, faster and

𝑘𝑘𝑘𝑘

2

intuitive compared to generic remote sensing technologies.

• Sentinel 2 imagery, which has relevant resolution for wildfire analysis, is publicly available.

• The useability of the technology and the accessibility of data will help local government

workforce implement wildfire damage assessment efficiently.

Conclusion

• High-resolution Sentinel-2 imagery was acquired using the Copernicus Browser, which is publicly

available web service, collecting false color composite bands.

• In segmentation step, a trial and error of multiple spatial, spectral, and minimum cell size inputs

were conducted until the correct balance was discovered to categorize the best segmented pixels

into reasonable classes.

• Large numbers of training sample objects were collected to refine the accuracy and to

differentiate between similar segmented objects.

• The SVM classification resulted a overall accuracies and Kappa percentage greater than 90

percent for both images.

• A comparison of polygon areas revealed an approximate forest land cover change of 64 %.

• In the final results, Lowland Grassy Woodland in the South East Corner Bioregion was found to

Black and White Infrared: Panchromatic film exposed as a negative

Color Positive: Color film exposed as a positive (often referred to
as slide film)

as visible light.

Best Practices for Digitizing Aerial Photography and
Physical Geospatial Media

Link:
https://www.asprs.org/common/Uploaded%20files/Standards/DPAC/Best_Practices_for_Digitizing_Aeri
al_Photography_and_Physical_Geospatial_Media.pdf

Key components:
• Storage and Handling
• Hardware
• Planning
• Digitizing/Scanning Process

• Formats
• Resolutions and Recommendations
• Radiometry

• Storage
• Metadata

Scanning Hardware
Photogrammetric Scanners:
Minimize distortion and use precise
calibration for sub-pixel accuracy in
measurements.

Commercial Scanners:
Offer high native resolution suitable for
digitizing photographic prints and paper
maps.

File Formats

Recommended format

• Use a common, universally compatible format: TIFF is recommended over PNG and other formats

• Never use RAW/proprietary formats.

•

Prefer tiled TIFF when supported (tile sizes 256 or 512) for faster loading/navigation.

Compression

• Do not use JPEG or other lossy formats as the sole scanning format (JPEG copies acceptable for viewing).

•

•

If storage constraints require lossy compression, TIFF with JPEG compression is recommended.  Use
minimal compression: JPEG quality ~90/100 (or 11/12).

Consider lossless alternatives (Deflate, JPEG2000) and document/confirm workflow compatibility.

File Formats

Bit Depth

•

Bit depth = data stored per pixel; impacts tonal range and feature interpretation.

• Guideline: scanner should capture at least 11 bits/pixel to preserve dynamic range (especially for

•

•

photogrammetric applications).

8-bit may be an acceptable compromise for visualization-only use cases.

If limited to 8-bit scanners/services, scanning is still better than none; consider light falloff correction
algorithms.

Color Storage

•

•

Archive scans in RGB for compatibility.

Avoid CMYK (primarily for printing; not ideal for archival display/storage).

 Pyramid Files

•

•

•

Pyramids (reduced-resolution overviews) improve performance; may be embedded or separate.

They increase file size but help in many software packages.

Can be generated by scanning software or tools like GDAL / GIS software.

Image Resolution Definitions
DPI = number of pixels per inch
Photogrammetric scanners often use micron size (pixel size in micrometers).
Conversion: DPI = 25,400 / micron size (e.g., 12.5 µm = \~2023 DPI).
Higher DPI decreases Ground Sample Distance (GSD), improving the effective ground resolution — but only up to
the limits of the film.

•
•
•
•

• GSD = Scale ÷ DPI
• GSD values are approximate unless the true scale is known or triangulated per frame
•

Examples:

•
•

1"=500 ft at 2000 DPI → 0.25 ft GSD
1"=1000 m at 1000 DPI → 1 m GSD

Panchromatic film: silver halide crystals 0.2–2.0 µm; clumping increases visible grain.
Color film: dye clouds 10–25 µm, especially large in older films → more noise.

Film Grain Considerations
•
•
• Grain becomes a limiting factor: scanning finer than the grain yields no benefit.
If scans appear noisy or overly grainy, lowering resolution may improve usability.
•

Practical Guidance
•
•
•

Test scans are essential for finding the optimal resolution.
Scanning below \~20 µm (\~1270 DPI) may approach the size of color dye clouds.
For high-quality film, scanning at **12.5 µm (\~2000 DPI)** is often ideal.

Image Resolution Recommendations

M E D I A   T Y P E

R E C O M M E N D E D   R E S O L U T I O N

Large Format Panchromatic Negatives, Diapositives, Duplicate Film (pre-1980)

14 Microns (~1800 DPI) minimum, 12.5 Microns (~2000 DPI) maximum

Modern Large Format Panchromatic Film (post-1980)

14 Microns (~1800 DPI) minimum, 12.5 Microns (~2000 DPI), 10 Microns (~2500
DPI) maximum

Large Format Color Film (pre-1980)

14 Microns (~1800 DPI)

Modern Large Format Color Film (post-1980)

14 Microns (~1800 DPI) minimum, 12.5 Microns (~2000 DPI) maximum

Small Format Panchromatic Motion Picture Film

12.5 Microns (~2000 DPI) minimum, 10 Microns (~2500 DPI) maximum

Small Format Color Motion Picture Film

12.5 Microns (~2000 DPI) minimum, 10 Microns (~2500 DPI) maximum

Glass Plate

Mylar Image

Mylar Map

Photographic Paper (Prints)

Paper Maps

15 Microns (~1700 DPI)

12.5 – 15 Microns (~2000 – 1700 DPI)

20 Microns (1270 DPI)

12.5 Microns (~2000 DPI)

20 Microns (1270 DPI)

Radiometric Suggestions

Definitions
Radiometry refers to balancing color, brightness, and contrast during scanning to preserve all information in the aerial
imagery for use in visualization and photogrammetry.
Pixel values (Digital Numbers, DN) range from 0–255 (8-bit) or 0–65535 (16-bit) and represent image tonal information

B&W Imagery
• Never scan in “true black & white.” This produces only 0 or 1 values and destroys image detail. Use

•
•

•
•

Grayscale/Panchromatic settings instead.
A proper scan shows a bell-shaped histogram with minimal pixels at 0 or 255 to avoid data loss.
Borders or fiducial marks naturally contain true blacks/whites—exclude these areas when evaluating histograms (use
~90% of image in calculating the histogram).
Avoid histogram stretching on 8-bit images to prevent data loss.
Highly uniform or extreme scenes (dense forest, bright urban, desert) may not show a typical histogram shape—this
is expected.

Radiometric Suggestions

Proper Balancing

Proper Balancing

Radiometric Suggestions

Histograms for each RGB band should be well-balanced without clipping at high/low ends.
Apply white balance (automatic or manual) to avoid color casts (tint).

Color Imagery
•
•
• Use a color-calibrated monitor and avoid fluorescent lighting during evaluation.
•

Color negatives often have a red base; best results require scanners with variable RGB light sources.

Optimization & Post-Processing
•

Contract work may require specific DN ranges, but for archival preservation this is not mandatory since this may
require multiple scans to achieve.
Limit post-processing unless necessary.  The goal is to preserve original radiometry for future photogrammetric
and other uses.
For films with heavy vignetting, scan at 11–14 bits and optionally run auto-balancing tools before saving a
secondary 8-bit version.

•

•

Digitization Area
•
•

Always digitize all of the media (film, paper, etc.): include edges, notes, borders, film fiducials and exposure info.
These Metadata areas may be scanned at lower resolution than the main image.

Metadata

For the Metadata, the committee focused on just the metadata surrounding the digitization of the media,
understanding that not all information about the original collection of the imagery or data will be available

name: David Hughes
markdown:
An Approach for Automated Change Detection From Satellite Imagery Spectra
David Hughes, UT-Battelle

Applied Image Processing, Analysis, and Classification
Abstract #: 217505
3:00-3:15pm MT
Bluebird Ballroom 3E

A graph approach for agnostic change
using passive spectral sensing.

How to determine general composite
change without having a priori info on
the target or background?

(1) Reducing imagery to spend

compute time on specific areas
(2) Applicability to MSI and comparing

to HSI

(3) Challenges and ways forward such
      as bulk graph analysis

Baga, Nigeria
(January 2015)

Five Reasons To Perform This
Work?

•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•

Existing approaches

Short label

Detail

Linear vs non-linear

Spectral unmixing is not a linear problem; linear fits are rough approximations.

Unending training set
creation/adaption

Random forest, deep learning, some non-linear mapping algorithm rely on training for
a set of known endmembers to search.

Not using full spectra

Rely on spatial layer differencing, band ratios, and index combinations not utilizing full
spectrum collected.  Such methods introduce errors from compounding detection
estimates and bias change with simplification.

Coarse resolution

Detects coarse land use change.  Spatial resolution to perform detection (10-30 m).

Noisy result

Image quality

Detects significant change that is not important such as coastal fluctuations and non-
germane environmental changes.

Quantitative performance metrics for detection, classification provided including all
quality factors (cloud, water, shadow).

Cross-modal but not really All results kept separate (e.g. SAR and MSI).

Graph Approach

o

Executed on tiles, not pixels, on a multispectral (e.g. WorldView) or desirably
hyperspectral (e.g. commercial or NEON airborne).

o Map all the spectral values of all pixels in the tile and notice how clusters form in
the hyperplane (Figure 1).  Demarcate these clusters as similar objects.  Natural
objects (normal distributions) and manmade materials (outliers) appear different
in the hyperplane.

o

Figure 2, Construct for all clusters and find the inflection which define two
regions where the tail represents outliers to clusters (typically non-natural
materials).

Figure 1.  Exemplar hyperplane
visualization

Define “significant change” as a varied length in the tail over
