Esri is ready
Here’s what you can expect…

• We will support the NSRS 2022 Modernization!

- including continued support for all existing coordinate systems, geoid models, and

transformations, including U.S. Survey Feet as a valid unit of linear measure

• We have been following the NGS’s work on NSRS 2022 since its inception

- attending and presenting at industry partner summits

- testing as interim products have been released

• We will NOT release any definitions in production software until final certification

Esri is ready
Some anticipated challenges…

• Adequate understanding by all stakeholders

- From executive leadership to production teams, understanding is key

• Stakeholders evaluating and transforming legacy data

- Making educational resources available

- Simplifying the process to the extent practical

• Ensuring data fidelity, now and always!

- Metadata

• People and processes, not technology

Esri is ready
What’s ahead in 2026…

• We will continue working closely with NGS

- Testing and integrating as the NGS continues to release NSRS products

• Educating our customers through events

- Esri UC – July 13-17, 2026, San Diego, CA

• Continuing to develop resources and content

Thank you!

name: Lance Phy
markdown:
Validation of Automated Aerial Photogrammetry
using RealityCaptureScan

Daniel Cruz, Lance Phy – Aperture LLC

ASPRS GEOWeek – February 17, 2026

About Us – Lance Phy

Managing Expert – Accident Reconstruction

Lance Phy is an Accident Reconstructionist with Aperture.  Lance
specializes in the investigation, reconstruction, and analysis of
motorcycle, passenger car, and commercial vehicle crashes. Lance has
over 30 years of experience in this field and shifted his focus
specifically to accident reconstruction in 2005. Over the course of his
career, Lance has investigated more than 4,000 crashes and has
testified in both state and federal courts across the country.

About Us – Daniel Cruz

Case Analyst – Accident Reconstruction

Pioneering workflow development in RealityScan for over 2 years.
Leading day-to-day operations in processing forensic scene and scan
data presented to clients, including roadway evidence and vehicle 3D
modeling from scan and photogrammetric data. Developed company-
wide 3D development workflow procedures including but not limited
to best practices, standard operating procedures, step-by-step
guides/tutorials, and various protocols.

Forensically investigated hundreds of litigation cases, regularly
conducting research and testing of cutting-edge geospatial and
geolocation methods.

Introduction

The use of automated aerial photogrammetry in crash reconstruction is well established
and nearly ubiquitous in the industry

We have explored RealityScan (formerly RealityCapture) as a tool for automated
photogrammetry from an RTK-featured small unmanned aerial vehicle

The aerial RTK feature and RealtyScan process produces reasonably accurate results of
an aerial survey of the ground plane without the reliance of ground control points

Using an RTK-only workflow minimizes risk and maximizes safety while conducting open
roadway data collection

Comparative analysis and validation of horizontal geometry and scale fidelity between
GCP-Controlled and Real-Time Kinematic reconstruction methods

Purpose of Testing

Validation of RealityScan in accident reconstruction data processing

Data Collection

Two FARO Focus 3D Laser Scanners - Scanning 1000-ft span of Houston, TX roadway

Autel Evo II RTK Series V3 Drone – 6 flights surveying 1000-ft

EMLID Reach RS2+ GNSS Receiver - Capturing ground control point positioning

Measuring wheels – mapping out 100-ft increments across 1000-ft

Ten ground control point markers - Staked out across 1000-ft

Autel Drone & EMLID GNSS Receiver – FIX corrections via Trimble® RTKNet.com NTRIP
Server on NAD83(2011) – Texas South Central us-ft Datum

Data Collection

Data Processing

FARO Scan Processing: FARO Scene > RealityCapture Reconstruction > Orthographic Render

Data Processing

Aerial Survey Processing: Import Images > Photogrammetric & Georeferenced Alignment > Mesh Render > Orthographic Render

Flight parameters: 100ft Elevation | Cross-Grid Pattern | Pitch Angle 70° below-horizontal | FIX NTRIP Corrections

Conclusions

The use of automated aerial photogrammetry in crash reconstruction provides reliable
data for measurements of roadway features and crash-related evidence

RealityScan is a reliable tool for automated photogrammetry from an RTK-featured small
unmanned aerial vehicle

For crash reconstruction applications, the aerial RTK feature and RealtyScan process
produces acceptably accurate results of an aerial survey of the ground plane without the
reliance of ground control points

Using an RTK-only workflow minimizes risk and maximizes safety while conducting open
roadway data collection

The results are beautiful

BLUF and Background
• Problem: Many hazardous grade crossings lack vertical profile data; low-clearance risks are often unclassified and rely
on manual inspection or limited field studies. This information is critical for route planning of low clearance vehicles.

• Gap: Despite widespread availability of high-resolution lidar systematic, automated evaluation of crossing vertical

geometry remains limited.

• Approach: An automated lidar-based workflow integrates classified point clouds with road and rail vectors to extract

dense elevation profiles at intersections.

• Metric: A unified clearance metric based on AASHTO tolerances classifies crossings as at-grade, compliant or low-

clearance/superelevated.

•

Impact: Lidar-derived profiles identify hazardous geometry not captured in existing inventories, enabling scalable,
data-driven safety assessment.

Metrics and Guidance: "To prevent drivers of low-clearance vehicles from becoming caught on the tracks, the crossing
surface should be at the same plane as the top of the rails for a distance of 2 ft [0.6 m] outside the rails. The surface of
the roadway should also not be more than 3 in. [75 mm] higher or lower than the top of nearest rail at a point 30 ft [9 m]
from the rail unless track superelevation makes a different level appropriate, as shown in Figure 9-66. Vertical curves
should be used to traverse from the roadway grade to a level plane at the elevation of the rails. Rails that are
superelevated, or a roadway approach section that is not level, need a site-specific analysis for rail clearances.“

AASHTO Green Book (2018), A Policy on Geometric Design of Highways and Streets, 7th ed., §9.12.2

Future Work and Considerations

•Roadway geometry :
Access to DOT-maintained 2D road planimetric data is required to quantify lane configurations, curb and gutter
geometry, shoulder widths, and traffic control element locations, enabling an accurate representation of
intersection structure and operational context.

•Direction-dependent hang-up risk:
Vehicle hang-up events at rail-highway crossings are not always symmetric and may occur in only one direction
of travel due to lane-specific vertical geometry, curb placement, or approach grade, while opposing lanes remain
passable.

•Lane-level modeling requirement:
Incorporating direction-of-travel at the lane level is necessary to resolve asymmetric hang-up conditions that are
obscured by centerline-based or bidirectional representations.

•Lidar currency and validity:
Clearance-grade calculations are highly sensitive to small vertical changes; reliance on decade-old lidar datasets
with varying quality levels introduces uncertainty, necessitating updated surveys to maintain analytical validity.

Convolutional Networks for Rainfall–Runoff Prediction in

A Comprehensive Analysis of Graph Convolutional Networks and Recurrent Neural Networks for Streamflow Prediction

→ Hydrological

Forecasting

Presented by:

Anil Kumar Mandal

Civil, Environmental and Geomatics Engineering

Florida Atlantic University

Research Motivation and Problem Statement

The Critical Challenge

Traditional rainfall-runoff models lack real-time adaptability for
extreme weather events, creating critical gaps in emergency response
planning.

Key Problems:

Floods are the most damaging natural hazard, and their risks are
rising with climate change (Besaw et al., 2010; KC et al., 2023; Kazadi

et al., 2024).

Rainfall–runoff prediction is crucial for managing water systems

and reducing storm-related impacts (Besaw et al., 2010; Mandal et al.,

2025; Xue & Zhu, 2025).

Timely streamflow forecasts are essential for effective early
warnings and emergency response (Luo et al., 2024; Shao et al.,
2025; Taghizadeh et al., 2025).

Hurricane Katrina damage

Research Motivation and Problem Statement

Overview

Physically based models (e.g., HEC-HMS, SWAT) use differential

equations to simulate rainfall–runoff processes (Besaw et al., 2010; Y.

Zhou et al., 2025). However, their practical use for real-time flood

forecasting is limited by three major drawbacks:

Key Challenges:

Computational Burden: These models are computationally
demanding, which makes them too slow for operational, real-time
forecasting (Besaw et al., 2010; Jiang et al., 2023).

High Calibration Demand: They require extensive and often

subjective calibration, and their parameters do not always transfer

reliably across different basins (Besaw et al., 2010; Jiang et al., 2023).

Non-Linearity Struggle: Their process-based structure often fails to
capture the highly non-linear and variable behavior of real-world
watersheds, especially in data-limited settings (Besaw et al., 2010;
Zhao et al., 2020).

Floodbase | Federal Flood Maps Are No Match for Florida’s Double Hurricane

Tabas et al. 2025

Research Motivation and Problem Statement

Overview

The growing availability of hydrometeorological data has accelerated the

adoption of data-driven methods for hydrologic forecasting.

Key Summary:

Early ML models (ANN, SVM) captured nonlinear patterns but
could not model temporal dependencies (Roudbari et al., 2023;
Luo et al., 2024).

RNNs (LSTM, GRU) improved forecasting by learning long-term

sequences (Xiang & Demir, 2021; R. Zhou, 2025).

Conventional RNNs assume a lumped watershed, relying only on
outlet data (Feng et al., 2021; Mosaffa et al., 2025).

They ignore spatial processes such as rainfall distribution and
upstream–downstream flow, limiting accuracy (Feng et al., 2021; Y.
Zhou et al., 2025).

Dbhydro

Xu et al., 2025

Objective

Graph

Convolutional

Network

→

Recurrent

Architecture

→ Hydrological

Forecasting

Develop a high-resolution, event-based STGCN framework that accurately captures watershed flow dynamics
and supports real-time flood forecasting.

• Develop an event-based STGCN framework capable of learning high-resolution rainfall–runoff dynamics

•

•

from more than 200 storm events across five connected HUC12 basins.
Integrate hydrologically informed directed graphs that encode the true upstream–downstream routing
structure of the Kissimmee River system, enabling more realistic spatial learning compared to generic k-
NN graphs.
Enhance flood-peak prediction performance by testing multiple recurrent backbones (LSTM, GRU,
SimpleRNN) and introducing a hybrid peak-weighted loss function tailored for extreme flow events.

markdown:
Tracking Illegal Mining and Deforestation
in Ghana’s Atewa Forest, Using Landsat
Imagery

Presenters: Ransford Tege and Brian Brokling

University of Wyoming

OUTLINE

Study Context

Research Gap

Data & Method

Results

Implication

Ghana: Physical Geography

• West Africa (4°–11°N; 3°W–1°E)

• Borders: Côte d’Ivoire (W), Burkina Faso (N), Togo (E), Atlantic
Ocean (S)

• Ecological zones:
  – Savanna Belt (Northern)

  – Forest zone (Middle Belt): Atewa & Akyem–Abuakwa forests
  – Coastal zone: lagoons, mangroves, sandy beaches

• Major water systems: Volta Basin, Pra Basin, Ankobra Basin

Natural Resources & Extraction pressures

• Agriculture: cocoa (2nd largest producer in
Africa), food crops, fisheries
  – Employs ~33–40% of the national workforce

 •Mining: gold, diamonds, bauxite, manganese
  – Gold = ~ 30-35% of export earnings

 – Expansion of artisanal and illegal mining

 – Growing encroachment into forest reserves

•Why Atewa Matters

 – Critical water catchment (Densu, Birim rivers)

 – High biodiversity

 – Increasing mining expansion

RESEARCH GAP

What We Know
 – Illegal mining drives forest degradation in Ghana.
 – Atewa has been politically contested (bauxite & gold).
 – Field reports document visible destruction

What We Do NOT Know:
 – The quantifiable spatial extent of mining-driven forest
loss over the past decade.
 – How rapidly vegetated areas are transitioning into bare
ground.
 – To quantify and map forest-to-bare-ground transitions using
multi-temporal Landsat imagery

Landsat 8 :2015 & Landsat 9: 2024 images;  30m Spatial resolution

•Supervised classification
•Change detection analysis
•Bare Ground Index calculation
•Vegetation-to-bare-ground transition mapping

Sample Training Points

Supervised Classification Results

Supervised Accuracy Assessments

2015

2024

Producers Accuracy

Producers Accuracy

— Disturbed 71.4%

— Built-Up 92.8%

— Forest 48.3%

— Disturbed 66.6%

— Built-Up 65.3%

— Forest 52%

Users Accuracy

Users Accuracy

— Disturbed 33.3%

— Built-Up 43.3%

— Forest 100%

— Disturbed 20%

— Built-Up 56%

— Forest 96%

Results

Major Findings:
— 11% of primary vegetated areas
converted to bare ground (2015–2024).

— Spatial expansion is not random
clustered near access corridors.

— Mining-driven land cover change is
accelerating.

— Bareground Index Increased 11.46%

𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆 + 𝑆𝑆 − (𝑁𝑁𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆 + 𝐵𝐵)
𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆 + 𝑆𝑆 − (𝑁𝑁𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆 + 𝐵𝐵)

Summary and conclusion

— Multi-temporal satellite analysis confirms
substantial forest-to-bare-ground transitions
in Atewa.

— The 11% conversion rate signals
accelerating ecological risk.

— Remote sensing data enables continuous
monitoring in politically sensitive landscapes.

— Findings contribute empirical evidence to
national debates on mining governance and
conservation.

summary: The study uses Landsat imagery to track the spatial extent of mining-driven forest loss in Ghana's Atewa Forest between 2015 and 2024, finding a significant increase in bare ground due to deforestation.

Introduction
Global Trends in Agricultural Productivity

• Global agricultural output growth has reduced to ~1.9%

per year during 2011 - 2020.

• The graph highlights that global agricultural output

growth is slowing down, declining from 2.79% (1961 -

1970) to 1.94% (2011 - 2021).

• Total Factor Productivity (TFP) now drives most growth,

highlighting the need for smart, data-driven, and

resource-efficient farming practices to sustain future

productivity.
2/26/2026

Total Agricultural Output: Total quantity or value of all agricultural goods (crops + livestock) produced

TFP: How efficiently all inputs (land, labor, capital, materials, energy) are used
together to produce output

2

Introduction
Agricultural Productivity Trends in the United States

 U.S. farm output nearly tripled from 1948 to

2021,  while  input  use  remained  stable  or

declined.

 Productivity  improvements  (TFP),  better

use of land, labour, capital, and intermediate

inputs, are now the main driver of growth.

 This  trend  underlines  the  importance  of

data-driven  decision-making,

technology,

and  monitoring

in  modern  agriculture,

aligning with our research focus.

2/26/2026

Total farm inputs: Labor + Land + Capital + Intermediate goods (fertilizer, feed, energy, etc.)

any Native American tribe in the United States -

Goal of the Research Work -
Smart and Connected Farms (SCFs)

 Smart Systems: Use IoT devices, sensors, and

automation for real-time monitoring of soil,

crops, and weather.

 Remote Sensing: Employ drones and satellite

imagery for precise crop management and

resource optimization.

 Connectivity:  Extend  internet  coverage  to

rural areas for data access and communication.

2/26/2026

9

Literature Review

Training data quality & CDL reliability (Hao 2016, Zhang 2022, Li 2024)
 Historical CDL improves training sample reliability
 High-confidence pixels reduce ground-truth dependency
 Enables in-season and large-scale crop mapping

Sensor resolution & spectral features (Tran 2022, Guan 2018)
 Sentinel-2 (10 m) outperforms 30 m CDL products
 Spectral indices (NDVI, EVI, SWIR, LSWI) improve crop discrimination
 Better field boundary detection and crop separation

Automation & methodological trends (Mouad Alami 2023)
 Shift toward ML/DL and spatio-temporal fusion
 Multi-source data integration as future direction

2/26/2026

10

arid regions such as the Navajo Nation
 CDL  accuracy  constrained  by  mixed
reliable  ground
limited

pixels  and
samples

CDL pixels to improve crop classification
accuracy

Crop Calendar

Spectral Indices

The  four  VIs  with  their  formulas  are  shown  as
follows:
i) NDVI = (NIR−RED)/(NIR+RED)
ii) GCVI = NIR/GREEN−1
iii) EVI =
G×(NIR−RED)/(NIR+C1×RED−C2×BLUE+L)
iv) LSWI = (NIR−SWIR1)/(NIR+SWIR1)

 NDVI:

Indicates

photosynthetic
capacity  but  can  saturate  at  high
biomass (Sellers et al., 1992).

 GCVI:  Best  linear  relationship  with
LAI for corn and soybean (Gitelson et
al., 2003).

 EVI:  Reduces  atmospheric  effects  by
including  blue  bands  (Huete  et  al.,
2002).

Abbreviation:
NIR: Near-Infrared; SWIR: Short-Wave Infrared; G: Gain factor = 2.5; C1 & C2: Coefficients to
correct for atmospheric resistance (6 & 7.5, respectively); L: Canopy background adjustment = 1.

2/26/2026

18

 LSWI: Sensitive to canopy water and
soil  moisture,  using  the  SWIR  band
(Xiao et al., 2002).

23

Summary & Further Investigation

 Classification accuracy  reached 86.7%  for  2017  and  83.7%  for  2022,  showing improved

performance using high-confidence pixels compared to the original CDL.

 However, the crop classes are still mixed with other classes. It should be addressed by using

spectral  reflectance  values  for  each  crop  (Dry  Beans  vs  Potatoes,  Corn  vs  Pop  Orn  Corn),

which will provide better distinction between crop classes.

 Improving  the  crop  classification  by  integrating  independent  Validation  datasets  &

exploring the spectral indices and their spectral reflectance part, as well as machine learning

algorithms to enhance accuracy and resilience.

 These refinements will ensure that classification outputs are both accurate and reflective of

actual field conditions, ultimately supporting more reliable agricultural monitoring in the

name: Rachelle Lavariega
markdown:
Mapping Urban Expansion of Santiago de
Queretaro (Mexico) using multi-temporal
Landsat imagery

Presented By: Rachelle Lavariega

Courtesy of Google Images

Santiago de Queretaro, Mexico

Where is it?

Topographic Map

The city lies about 200–215 km (two hours) northwest de Ciudad de Mexico. The city is in a semi-arid valley but is
surrounded by rugged hills and elevated ridges. The geographical terrain creates a bowl-like valley shape, which
shapes the direction of urban expansion.

Santiago de Queretaro: Urbanization

• Urban population grow from about 540,000 in 1989 to

~1.40 million in 2025.

• By comparing national numbers and regional industry
numbers, Querétaro as one (in the top 5) of Mexico’s
fastest-growing industrial and urban regions.

•

•

• Mexico’s population growth (2010-2020): 12.8%
• Queretaro population growth (2010-2020): 29.57%
Santiago de Querétaro’s rapid urban growth occurred mostly
after the 2000’s, by high-tech manufacturing industries such
as aerospace and automotive.
The region hosts one of Mexico’s leading aerospace
clusters, located near the International Airport. The cluster
holds major firms such as Bombardier and Safran. This
cluster is identified as the country’s fastest-growing
aerospace hub.

Issues/complexities with Rapid
Urbanization

• Water stress: The Valle de Querétaro aquifer has a

documented recharge deficit. Due to historic groundwater
over-pumping led to water-table declines, and the city is
categorized as having high water-scarcity risk. The city’s
steep, hilly terrain adds another layer of difficulty for
pumping and distribution water evenly.

• Analysts frequently note that transport congestion, gaps in

public transit coverage, and lagging road infrastructure have
not kept pace with the city’s rapid spatial expansion,
resulting in uneven service reliability for residents,
including access to utilities and potable water.
The city also experiences intermittent electricity outages
during peak-demand months, reflecting stress on the
existing power-supply infrastructure.

•

Santiago de Queretaro: Areas where Urban
sprawl can be identified

Courtesy of Google Images

Satellite Images of Santiago de Queretaro

Unsupervised Classification

was created, and these

represented urban or

however, 44 classes were
ultimately retained
because the 64-class
configuration produced

Expand to 1989 to 2025 :

Following the same steps, the results were as follows,
Total Land Area that is Urban Growth: 18.96%
•
Total Land Area that is Urban Loss: 5.32%
•

•
Total Land Area that had no change: 75.70%
•

The years 1989, 2000, and 2020 were selected because they
correspond to census years, allowing population change data
to provide a contextual backdrop for interpreting patterns of
urban growth.

Legend:
Dark Pink: Urban Growth
Dark Green: Urban Loss
Pale Yellow & Light Green: No Change

Following the unsupervised classification for 1989, 2015, and 2025,
the analysis will be extended to include the years 2000 and 2020.

• The Normalized Difference Built-up Index (NDBI) will be calculated for

each year to provide additional information on built-up areas and to assess
how these results compare with the unsupervised classification. Because, in
the 1989 satellite image, agricultural and farming land appears dark and can
be confused with built-up areas, NDBI will be combined with a vegetation
index (NDVI) to distinguish vegetated land from developed land.

• These indices (NDBI combined with NDVI) will be applied to every year

(1989, 2000, 2015, 2020, 2025).

• To finalize the analysis, topographic data (e.g., DEM or slope) will be

incorporated to quantify the percentage of new urban development shifting
toward higher elevations, as lower-elevation areas are already largely built
up.

Next Steps

summary: This study maps the urban expansion of Santiago de Queretaro, Mexico, between 1989 and 2025 using multitemporal Landsat imagery and an unsupervised classification method, highlighting significant changes in urban and non-urban areas.

Introduction

using Deep Learning

Motivation & Problem

• Sentinel-2 lacks fine-scale flood features.

comparable.

STCN-SR (proposed model)

The proposed network architecture of Spatial Transformer-CNN Network for SR (STCN-SR).

• Enhances 10 m Sentinel-2 to 5 m comparable resolution.
• Combines CNN + Transformer {(local features) +  (global
context) }.
• Modules: Shallow Feature Extractor, STCB, SFT, Fusion, Up
sample.

• STCB: CNN + Spatial Transformer Blocks.
• SFT: Positional encoding + Multi-head self-attention.
• FFB : Combines multi-level features.
• Output: High-res tile reconstructed by up
sampling.

with Satellite Landsat Imagery
for Bald Eagle Habitat Prediction
in the Del Rio Springs Ecosystem

Noah Morales, Ronny Schroeder, Elise Anderson
Embry-Riddle Aeronautical University - Prescott

Overview

1. Study Area

2. Phase I – NDVI Dataset
Comparison

hypothesis

methodology
• Results and key findings

3. Phase II – Bald Eagle
Habitat Assessment

5. References

• Methodology

• Results and habitat
patterns

Study Area

Map insert adopted from Schroeder et al., 2025:
http://commons.erau.edu/sustainability-conference/2025/poster/34

Phase I

Validating UAS-based NDVI Data with Satellite
Landsat Imagery

Research Question

Question: How closely do Normalized Difference Vegetation
Index (NDVI) values derived from high-resolution MicaSense
UAS imagery correlate with Landsat-derived NDVI?

Hypothesis: Despite differences in sensor design, Landsat
and MicaSense imagery will exhibit strong agreement in
vegetation patterns and spectral signatures, with
discrepancies mainly attributable to spatial resolution

Data Sources

Source

Satellite
Imagery

Survey

(ERAU Alumnus)

0.00%

Methodology

1. Data Acquisition

• Landsat imagery (USGS)
• MicaSense imagery (DJI Matrice 300)

2. NDVI Generation

•  Compute NDVI for Landsat
• Compute NDVI for MicaSense

3. Spatial Alignment &
Standardization

•  Resample MicaSense NDVI to Landsat resolution
• Snap raster for pixel alignment and define ROI and clip both datasets

4. Comparative Analysis &
Validation

•  Difference Map and Assessment of Spatial Correspondence
• Interpretation of discrepancies

Results

Difference
Map

Results (cont.)

Assessment of Spatial
Correspondence

• Coefficient of Determination (R² ≈ 0.51)
shows moderate agreement, with about
half of the NDVI variability shared
between datasets

Results (cont.)

Visualization of issues with Pix4D’s image stitching over complex canopies and low-light conditions

Band Composite (5,3,2)

NDVI

Conclusion

•

Shadows (trees & structures) show
unusually high NDVI in MicaSense
imagery

•

Likely causes:

• Apparent sensor saturation in

shaded/low light areas

• High sensitivity of UAV multispectral
imagery to changes in viewing angle
This results in unusually large NDVI
gradients when Pix4D stitches the
individual images together

•

MicaSense hot spot analysis overlayed with MicaSense NDVI
Band Composite (5,3,2)

Conclusion (cont.)

• MicaSense UAS largely overestimated NDVI and
Landsat NDVI outputs do not fully correspond​

•

•

Low spatial correspondence is likely due to
changes in viewing angle and lighting variability​

Landsat Level 1 data does not account for
atmospheric interference​

• Datasets cannot fully validate each other​

Conclusion (cont.)

• MicaSense UAS offers much higher spatial

resolution than Landsat

• Higher detail of UAS imagery enables more
precise mapping of vegetation patterns and
local habitat

• Useful depending on the scope of the

project and its application

•

You cannot take the accuracy of UAS-derived
NDVI data for granted

Phase II

Bald Eagle Habitat Mapping with MicaSense Data

Project Statement

Goal: Apply MicaSense NDVI data to map bald eagle
habitats

Key Insight: Dense tree clusters correspond to nesting
hotspots; sparsely vegetated areas indicate foraging
zones.

Methodology

1. Data Processing
(ArcGIS Pro)

•  Calculate NDVI from MicaSense

2. Spatial Analysis

•  Resample NDVI to 1-meter resolution raster
• Convert raster to point features and run Hot Spot Analysis

3. Interpretation &
Application

•  Analyze hot and cold spots to identify optimal eagle habitat

nesting and hunting locations

Results

Vegetation
Mapping and
Hotspot
Analysis

Discussion & Conclusion

• High-resolution hotspot analysis reveals precise
clusters of elevated NDVI, representing dense
canopy cover

• These vegetation hotspots indicate likely eagle
nesting habitat and improve the accuracy of
habitat suitability modeling by pinpointing
where nests are most likely to occur

• Areas outside hotspot clusters (NDVI cold spots)
represent sparsely vegetated or open terrain,
which are more consistent with eagle hunting
and foraging behavior

MicaSense hot spot analysis overlayed with MicaSense
NDVI raster imagery converted to point features

Conclusion (cont.)

• Hot and cold spot analysis from multispectral

UAS imagery predicts eagle nesting and
foraging zones.

• Hotspots indicate dense healthy, dense

vegetation suitable for nesting; cold spots
indicate open foraging areas.

• High-resolution UAS data enhances

environmental monitoring through detailed
vegetation analysis.

Future Steps

Field Validation:
Conduct on-site surveys to
validate predicted vegetation
hotspots and cold spots used
for nesting and foraging

Flight Alignment:
Time MicaSense flights to align
with Landsat overpasses to
ensure consistent lighting and
viewing conditions

Seasonal Vegetation Dynamics
& Stress Monitoring:
Use multi-season imagery to
assess habitat changes and
track vegetation stress
throughout the year

Level 2 Data Incorporation:
Utilize Level 2 Landsat data to
correct for atmospheric
interference

References

• OneSoil. :What the NDVI Index Is and How It Makes a Farmer’s Life  Easier.”
Medium, 11 May 2019, https://medium.com/onesoil/what-the-ndvi-
index-is-and-how-it-makes-a-farmers-life-easier-d6e900d91c9f. Image.

• Schroeder, Ronny, et al. “Advancing Environmental Monitoring at Verde River
Headwaters State Park through Integrated Remote Sensing
Technologies.” Scholarly Commons, 2025,
https://commons.erau.edu/sustainability-conference/2025/poster/34.

• USGS. “EarthExplorer - Home.” Earthexplorer.usgs.gov, 2024,

https://earthexplorer.usgs.gov.

Thank You

Restore lake level to 978.5 MSL, enabling regional and
collegiate rowing teams to return—reconnecting
athletes with a Lake Lure tradition dating back to 1927

May 1, 2026
Reach full pond (990.5 MSL) and officially reopen Lake
Lure for public use, marking a major milestone in the
community’s full recovery

Laboratory, USDA, Houma, LA.

Geomatics Program, Now with DOTD, Baton Rouge, LA.

League, Thibodaux, LA.

TALK OVERVIEW

Background

Introduction

Research Objectives

Sensor and Platform Configuration

Data Preprocessing

Data Analysis

Results

Future Work

BACKGROUND

 Louisiana produces sugarcane on over 500,000 acres

of cultivated land across 24 parishes (counties).

 Each year the state produces over 15 million tons of
cane with an economic impact of $2.6 billion to the
sugarcane farmers and raw sugar factories which
translates to producing about 20% of the sugar grown
in the United States.

 More than a third of Louisiana parishes are either

directly or indirectly impacted by sugarcane economy.

INTRODUCTION

 To accurately determine a starting date, it is

necessary to obtain pre-harvest yield estimates of all
the acres that will be processed by a given mill

 Current methods to estimate yields rely on historical

yield data and visual estimates which are subject to a
high degree of error

 Obtaining accurate estimates of crop sucrose levels
could help growers develop harvest plans that would
target areas that have reached maturity, while
delaying harvest of areas that are not yet mature

RESEARCH OBJECTIVES

 Project research objectives involve capturing two

important events in the sugarcane growth stages for
accurate predication of sugarcane content
 Biomass change which is reflected by peak chlorophyll content

determined by spectral signature and crop structure (height and
plant density) determined by lidar point clouds

 Sucrose accumulation in the plant as evident by the yellowing

of the leaves (ripening of the stalks) is determined by the
diminishing chlorophyll content from the leaf to the plant.

CONCLUSIONS

 Data from our studies suggest that hyperspectral and
lidar data collected with UASs may offer a promising,
cost-effective method to estimate sugarcane yields
prior to harvest.

 The Vogelmann Red Edge Index 2 derived from

hyperspectral data showed a strong relation to sugar
yields

 Lidar data offers a promising alternative to predict

biomass yields.

FUTURE WORK

 Additional Lidar and Hyperspectral datasets on new

varieties will facilitate an accurate model to predict cane
and sugar yields
 Plant Senescence Reflectance Index (PSRI), Carotenoid Reflectance
Index (CRI1), Structure Insensitive Pigment Index (SIPI), Red Green
Ratio Index (RGRI), Normalized Difference Nitrogen Index (NDNI),
Disease Water Stress Index (DWSI), Leaf Water Vegetation Index1&2,
and Moisture Stress Index (MSI).

 Expanding the hyperspectral range to 1700NM will facilitate

determining water, carbon and  nitrogen content in plant foliage
 Potential opportunities exist to identify sugarcane diseases,

pests, and nutrient deficiencies with the adoption of
hyperspectral sensors

ACKNOWLEDGMENTS

 This research project was funded by

American Sugarcane League, Thibodaux,
LA and USDA/ARS, Sugarcane Research
Unit, Houma, LA. We would like to thank

Landry, Remote Pilots Mr. Peter Jansen
(Edison Chouest Offshore) and Mr. Mert
Pellegrin (Houma Terrebonne Airport
Commission) and Sugarcane Farmer Mr.
Keith Dugas.

markdown:
Transforming SCAT
with UAS: Oil Spill
Response in Marsh
Environments

Jennifer Horsman, GISP, Research Planning, Inc.

Acknowledgements

NOAA Office of Response and Restoration
Emergency Response Division

US Coast Guard Sector New Orleans

Louisiana Oil Spill Coordinators Office

Louisiana Department of Wildlife and Fisheries

US Fish and Wildlife Service

What is SCAT?

Shoreline

Cleanup

Assessment

Technique

Well, yes, but…

What is a SCAT Program?

• A systematic approach that:
−uses standard methods and

terminology

−to collect data on shoreline

and wetlands oiling conditions

−to support decision-making

for shoreline cleanup

General
SCAT
Process
Flowchart

Presentation will focus
on the initial phase of SCAT
for oil spill response circled in red

SCAT Activities

1. Reconnaissance survey
2. Developing a SCAT plan

1. Segment shoreline
2. Develop cleanup guidelines and endpoints
3. Pre-survey planning and team assignments

3. Shoreline surveys

SCAT Activities

4. Generate treatment recommendations
5. Monitoring cleanup operations
6. Post-treatment inspections
7. Final sign-off of cleanup activities

Reconnaissance Survey

• Objectives:

• Get an overall perspective

on shoreline types

• Determine the extent of
oiling on the shoreline

• Identify logistical constraints for shoreline
access for both SCAT and cleanup teams

Traditional SCAT Recon

• Fly entire impact area at 400-500
feet, 70-80 knots in helo or high-
wing aircraft

• Use GPS or charts to record:

• Flight path, date and time
• General degree of shoreline oiling
• References to photos/video taken

Traditional SCAT Recon

• Data & images are
shared with the
Command Post after
the aircraft returns
at the end of the
day and often not
reviewed until the
next day

Traditional SCAT Survey

• Many resources needed

• Multiple field teams of 2-4 people
more people in the field = higher
potential risk

• Boats and other vehicles
• Safety gear

• Takes time and money to order and allocate
resources and must be decontaminated after

UAS SCAT Survey

Always take the drone
to a response!

UAS SCAT Survey

Divisions

• Impact area is split into Divisions
that contain segmented shoreline

• Create flight areas within

divisions (can be edited on the fly)
• Lawnmower pattern with 80% front

and 70% side overlap

• One set of batteries per flight
• Overlap between flight areas

segmented shoreline

UAS SCAT Survey

vs. traditional SCAT survey

• No more paper forms!
• Fewer resources needed

and more quickly deployed
• Logistics generally smaller
• Better access to sensitive
or difficult-to-reach areas

UAS SCAT Survey

difficult to penetrate the
marsh vegetation by boat,
so surveys typically only
cover the outer edge

• UAS surveys cover more

area and allow seeing into
the marsh beyond the edge

air

UAS SCAT - Well 59 Incident

• 43+ shoreline miles surveyed
• 57 flights
• 8 days
• 4,455 acres
• Approximately 20K nadir photos

Conclusions

Using UAS for SCAT…

• Provides a better view of the potential impact area
• Protects the marsh from foot and boat traffic
• Is less invasive to sensitive sites such as alligator nests
• Removes risk of potential exposure to hazards such as

bubbling gas from a leak in a pipeline

• Is relatively quick to deploy and requires fewer resources

and logistics

• Can get information more quickly into the COP

ns

Let me teach you about geophysical
surveys and magnetometry!!!

What is Magnetometry?

• Principle of measuring the
Earth’s magnetic field

• Also… measuring the magnetic
anomaly of natural and man-
made features
• The amount of iron in

certain rocks

• Metal pipes
• Tanks

Unit of
Measurement

• Nanotesla (nT)
• A measure of magnetic strength
• The Earth's magnetic field strength
at the surface typically ranges from
25,000 to 65,000 nanoteslas (nT)
• Not a linear unit of measurement

(hard for a surveyor to understand!)

Magnetometry
Applications in
Geology, Mining,
Utilities, UXO
• Locating ore bodies
• Faults
• Metallic Objects
• Unexploded Ordinance

Case Study:
Geology

• Locating faults and structures
• Mineral County, NV.
• Wassuk Mountain Range
• Site Borders the Hawthorne Army

Weapons Depot property

• Claims are on BLM federal lands

Walker Lake

Hawthorne

Project Site

Case Study:
Geology

• Big Indian Mine
• Old Injun Vein, Spanish Vein,

Altogether Vein, North Star Vein

• East-West Faults
• Gold and Silver Mined in 1920’s-

30’s

SkyHub

Altimeter

Magnetometer

MagNIMBUS

• SkyHub (brains)
• QuSpin GTFM Gen-2
• Fluxgate sensor
• Sensitivity <0.1nT
• Data Rate:
• Radar altimeter
• UgCS real-time altimeter and

≤

1000 samples/sec

magnetometer

Mission Planning

• Project site = ~125 acres
• DJI M350 drone
• SPH Engineering MagNIMBUS

sensor

• UgCS flight control app
• Flight lines parallel to slope and/or

perpendicular to linear feature
• 5m altitude, 5m line spacing, 5m/s

speed

• 30m altitude, 30m line spacing, 1m/s

speed

Data Collection

• Two field days
• ~125 acres mapped
• ~23 line km (14.5 mi) flown
• ~1500’ vertical relief (8500’-10,000’)
• ~12 flights total (20 min/flight)

Post-Processing

• Oasis montaj

• Database driven software
• Corrects and filters sources of noise

and error

• GNSS heading lag
• Drone motor electromagnetic

interference
• Stationary data
• Magnetic base station (diurnal

change)

Case Study:
Results

• Data results show several areas of
high and low magnetic anomalies
• Consistent with ground data, rock

samples, historic records, etc.

Case Study:
Geology

• Data highlighted previously

unknown lengths/extensions of
structures

• Filter and processing algorithms
allow for highlighting of high and
low magnetic readings along
different rock types… aka fault lines

Next Steps

Drilling and Modelling

Conclusions

• Drone and drone-based sensors can offer economical

advantages vs. crewed aircraft survey mission.

• Drone based sensors collect higher resolution data, enabling

better detection of objects and geologic structures.

• Drone based surveys are highly localized in area.

• Crewed aircraft surveys are more regional in size and scope.

• Aerial surveys are a broad paint brush, helping pinpoint further

investigation.

• Steep learning curve to new technology, but if you invest the

time and effort, there can be a huge benefit.

CONCLUSIONS

 High spatial and spectral resolution is needed to

extract diverse wetland plant species
community with similar spectral responses.
 SAM classification technique shows promise for

classifying hyperspectral image cubes.

 Dense lidar data has allowed us to resolve the
elevation changes from the crown of a forested
ridge to the adjacent intertidal marsh.

 Incorporate PIKA-NIR (900 – 1700NM) to map

 Data dimensionality reduction using PCA and

MNF techniques

 Working on AI/ML/DL techniques for targeted
species extraction and accurate DSM models
 Improve endurance times and coverage area

offered by the current airframe from 15 minutes
to 2-5 hours by employing a fuel cell drones

Agenda

Adjustment of exterior orientation parameters with

The trajectories are highly accurate three-dimensional points somewhere on the pavement

~1.75m to 2.45m

~0.11m to 1.00m

Pose points also eliminate road shear

Improve image pointing accuracy and provide correct road elevation

Ortho images
before correction

Ortho images
after correction

Why are the pose point powerful?

A standard photogrammetric approach based on GCP can only be done on a small scale.
However, the mobile pose point approach provides a near global solution world-wide

Pose points provide more comprehensive control.

Solution

HVA:  Susurluk, Turkey

How are the mobile pose points used to improve pointing accuracy

 Standard photogrammetric solutions involve photo-identifiable ground control points (GCPs) with

reliable and highly accurate ground coordinates.

 The mobile pose points represent highly accurate 3D coordinates somewhere on the pavement.

− They are NOT photo-identifiable, therefore the photogrammetric techniques will not work.

 Vantor has invented a new method, based on Information Theory, to improve pointing accuracy of

satellite imagery.
− US patent pending

 We use these dense, highly accurate three-dimensional points created by mobile mapping as

control.

 The application is proven.
 It can improve product objectives to sub-meter absolute accuracy and co-registration.
 It can reduce shear and minimize road breaks.
 It can more than fill the gap of GPCs.

Shift Happens – Rethinking Geospatial in the Fourth
Dimension

Evon Silvia, PLS

Technical Program Manager, NV5

GeoWeek 2026 – Denver, Colorado – February 18, 2026

Outline

Intro: An evolving relationship with time

Three adventures in working with/around/against time
1. Exploding points
2. Temporal divides
3. The blessing and curse of labels

Temporal best practices for…
1. Working in the west
2. Planning large projects spanning multiple years
3. Preparing your data holdings for NATRF2022+NAPGD2022

An evolving relationship with time

Remove time so that we can document the nation
• NAD27 & NGVD29, NAD83 & NAVD88
• Emphasis on physical benchmarks
• Relative measurements
• Boundary surveying, property sale/transfer, construction sites

Measure features over time so that we can update
• NAD83(HARN), NAD83(2011)(Epoch2010.00), WGS84 & ITRF
• NGS CORS network, SCIGN, Earthscope/UNAVCO
• Absolute positioning
• Mapping & GIS for larger regions for detecting trends

Model time so that we can adapt
• NATRF2022 & NAPGD2022, WKTv2, LAS 1.5
• 14-parameter transformations, HTDP & IFDM2022, localized modeling
• Global positioning, autonomous navigation
• Change detection, long term modeling, predictions at any scale

Adventures with time
Case Study 1 – Exploding points

Exploding points near the southern border during
transformation from ITRF08(Epoch2014.25) to
NAD83(2011)(Epoch2010.00)

Isolated to div-0 issues bordering an earthquake

Investigation

• Generate a 1-2km grid across the project area
• Transform that grid 3 ways using HTDP

1. Full HTDP transformation, with datum & epoch transformation
2. Datum transformation only
3. Epoch transformation only

• Map the dx, dy, and dz results to reveal trends and discontinuities

Where did it come from?

Where did it come from?

Where did it come from?

Outcomes

• Culprit: 7.2 El Mayor-Cucupah earthquake on April 4, 2010
• Solution: Transform to April 5, 2010 instead of January 1, 2010

Lessons learned from Case Study 1

• Use lightweight grid-based sampling to support troubleshooting

• There are multiple valid ways to use the existing tools

• Epoch transformations may result in unexpected behavior

• Think strategically about how to handle faults and earthquakes

Adventures with time
Case Study 2 – Temporal divides

Kilauea – 2m movement – 2018 eruption

California Coast Range – 30cm movement – normal motion

California Central Valley – 10+cm subsidence

• >4cm of movement in 6 months
• Regular updates of control network

Lessons learned from Case Study 2

• Projects under 100,000 acres can typically be efficiently transformed with block

shifts, which are faster than individually transforming points

• Plan ahead for how to handle differential movement for large projects

• Use lightweight grid-based sampling to develop a plan

• The problem already exists and is a dirty secret hidden in horizontal errors of lidar

Adventures with time
Case Study 3 – The blessing and curse of labels

Problematic NAD83(HARN)

• Two versions of NAD83(HARN) in many

states

• Labeling of NAD83(HARN) is

inconsistent…

 NAD83(1992/1996/etc)
 NAD83(HARN)
 NAD83(FBN)
 NAD83(CORS96)
 NAD83………

• NCAT provides the option to include
height changes. Should you use it?
• HARN itself includes many localized

adjustments to individual benchmarks
(more on this later)

What does “NAD83” mean?

• NAD83(86), which was 1:1 equivalent

to WGS84

• NAD83 “vanilla” (just a sticker?)

• NAD83 but unspecified realization for

GIS compatibility

• Does it even matter?

Mixed messaging from NOAA NGS

• Officially, you shouldn’t use HTDP to transform between NAD83 realizations; you

should use NCAT

• Nevertheless, OPUS and OPUS Projects did exactly this when transitioning from

NAD83(CORS96)(Epoch2002.00) to NAD83(2011)(Epoch2010.00)

Conclusion
• Any data tied to OPUS should be transformed based on epochs using HTDP
• Any data tied to physical benchmarks should be transformed with NCAT
• Often, the datum differences are sub-decimeter
• Important differences are temporal

Massive projects benefit from long term consistency

• Keep the reference frame consistent

for as long as you can

• Mapping projects should use tools
that are widely available, even if it
means sacrificing a couple of
centimeters
• OPUS
• NAD83, not WGS84
• HTDP
• Regional RTNs

• Favor global adjustments over local

adjustments

• Expect inconsistency between

subcontractors, efforts, and years 
plan significant

Lessons learned from Case Study 3

• You must be extremely familiar with the NGS tools to get the expected answer
• Sometimes the “correct” textbook approach is overruled by the project history
• NAD83(HARN) simply isn’t suitable for massive, wide area projects
• NAD83 “vanilla” and NAD83(HARN) mean different things to different people 

you must immediately start digging to learn more

It is better to be consistent than to be right

Best Practices for when
Shift Happens

How to work in the west

The distinctions
between NAD83
realizations are
important

Your software
might be confused
when it says
WGS84 = NAD83

Most software isn’t
modeling ITRF 
NAD83 correctly
west of the Rockies

Make sure your
project is big
enough to hold still

OPUS is King /
know your RTN

Update/verify your
control network
regularly

How to plan a large project for time

The “Golden
Dataset”

Pick an epoch /
reference date
(LAS 1.5)

Abandon
hydroflattening

Archive your LAZ,
not just DEMs

Plan significant
overlap

Your lidar survey
isn’t done until
you have done it
a second time

How to get ready for NATRF2022

Research your
projects to get more
detail about control
(epoch, realization,
geoid)

Demand LAS 1.5
compatibility from
software vendors

Find the source data
– transform the LAS,
not the DEMs

Engage with
specialists to
transform massive
datasets

Final words

The next generation of 4-dimensional remote sensing data is
about maximizing value by enabling us to model time.

Data collected for one purpose can move through time and
space so that it can be used for any other purpose.

This versatility is crucial for enabling autonomous real-time
navigation, change detection, AI applications, and prediction in
an increasingly uncertain world.

Thank you!

Evon Silvia

Technical Program Manager, NV5
evon.silvia@nv5.com
https://www.nv5.com/geospatial

LAS Working Group Chair, ASPRS
las@asprs.org

https://github.com/ASPRSorg/LAS

markdown:
Research Methods for Validation of
Lidar Surfaces in Complex Terrain

February 18, 2026
Geo Week

Jeff Irwin1, Ajit Sampath2, Mark Bauer3

1. U.S. Geological Survey (USGS) Earth Resources Observation and Science (EROS) Center
2. KBR, Contractor to USGS EROS
3. USGS National Uncrewed Systems Office (NUSO)

U.S. Department of the Interior
U.S. Geological Survey

Motivation & Validation Challenge
• Why This Study

 3DEP Lidar is increasingly used for terrain-dependent

applications

 Validation under complex terrain and canopy remains

operationally difficult

•

 Yet accuracy under these conditions is critical for

downstream use
Validation Challenge
 Dense canopy and understory obscure true ground
 Ground classifiers (especially TLS and UAS) may retain

understory as ground

 Simple minimum-Z or point-to-point checks are insufficient

Validating Geometric Quality of 3DEP Lidar Data in Complex Terrain
2
2

Probably not the best way
to measure VVA.  Photo
from a VVA checkpoint from
a 3DEP collection over
Puerto Rico.

Background

 USGS and USDA
FPAC funded two
pilots to coincidentally
collect QL1 lidar and
NAIP imagery in CO
and WA in 2023

 USGS EROS collected
ground validation data
in urban and
vegetated
vegetated areas within
both pilot AOIs

Validating Geometric Quality of 3DEP Lidar Data in Complex Terrain

3

Reference / Independent Measurements

•

Instruments

 Total Station (Trimble SX10) (CO, WA)
 GNSS (Trimble R10-2 base and

rovers)(open skies, grass, low vegetation)
(CO)

 TLS (Riegl VZ-400i) (CO, WA)
 UAS Lidar (YellowScan Vx-20 (Riegl

miniVUX-1UAV + Applanix APX-20 UAV))
(CO)
Key Assumption: Terrain is complex,
often under canopy, where validation is
hardest but most meaningful

Note: Any use of trade, firm, or product names is for descriptive
purposes only and does not imply endorsement by the U.S.
Government.

•

•

Validating Geometric Quality of 3DEP Lidar Data in Complex Terrain
4
4

Validation Philosophy
•

Primary Question: How well does 3DEP Lidar model
topography under complex terrain and canopy?
Validation Approach: Compare 3DEP surfaces against:
 Ground truth (Total Station, GNSS where applicable)
 Independent Lidar modalities (TLS, UAS)

•

• Guiding Principle

 Model the local terrain geometry before computing error

Another example from
Puerto Rico.

•

This reduces bias from:
 Noise
 Density variation
 Small spatial misalignments

Validating Geometric Quality of 3DEP Lidar Data in Complex Terrain
5
5

Modeling the Local Terrain Geometry
•

Plane-based surface estimation (PCA), not minimum-Z selection
Percentile filtering improves robustness (bottom 10%)
Explicit geometric quality controls (planarity + verticality checks)

Objective: Extract a statistically robust, terrain slope aware lowest
ground envelope from classified Lidar.
Key Takeaways: Local modeling instead of global filtering (0.5 m
tiles)
–
–
–
Big Idea: Ground filters, particularly on TLS data can leave
understory classified as ground.
Use a nonaggressive ground classifier and extract
validation candidate regions with a locally adaptive, plane-
constrained, percentile-based method that is statistically
stable and computationally scalable.

•

•

•

Validating Geometric Quality of 3DEP Lidar Data in Complex Terrain
6
6

Data Processing

•

•

•

Spatial Binning: Ground-class points divided into fixed XY
tiles (0.5 m resolution)
Incremental Tile Statistics: Streaming computation of per-
tile:
– Mean (μ)
– Covariance matrix (Σ)
PCA Surface Fitting
– Eigen-decomposition of Σ
– Smallest eigenvector → plane normal
– Quality thresholds: Planarity ratio (λ

/ Σλ < threshold), Minimum

vertical component (nᶻ > 0.3)

Validating Geometric Quality of 3DEP Lidar Data in Complex Terrain
7
7

₀

Data Processing

• Signed Distance Analysis: Compute point-to-plane

signed distances

• Percentile Thresholding: Per tile, retain bottom 10% of

negative distances
 Defines local lowest envelope

• Output: Filtered LAS representing statistically controlled

lowest ground surface

Validating Geometric Quality of 3DEP Lidar Data in Complex Terrain
8
8

Filtering the Validation Point Clouds

 Ground

Classified
Points

Validating Geometric Quality of 3DEP Lidar Data in Complex Terrain

9

 Unclassified
TLS Point
Cloud

 Filtered

Points for
Validation

Local Quadratic Surface Fitting for 3DEP vs TLS and UAS
Evaluation
 Objective: Estimate vertical error

between reference ground points and
test Lidar surfaces using local
quadratic surface modeling.

 Idea: Model the local terrain geometry

before computing error —
reduces bias from noise, density
variation, and small misalignments.
 Core Concept - Instead of direct
point-to-point comparison:
→ Fit a local quadratic surface in the
test (3DEP) dataset
→ Evaluate surface at the reference
point location
→ Compute vertical and 3D error
metrics

Validating Geometric Quality of 3DEP Lidar Data in Complex Terrain

10

• Surface Model is a Quadratic
surface: z = ax² + by² + cxy +
dx + ey + f

 Two-stage fitting:
• Eigen-based solution →
initial parameter estimate
• Nonlinear least squares
→ refined surface fit

• Error Metrics

 Z error → surface value
at evaluation point (0,0)

• Per-point records stored
with associated 3DEP file

 41 Total Station Points

Validating Geometric Quality of 3DEP Lidar Data in Complex Terrain
16
16

Total Station
vs 3DEP

Total Stations : Most Accurate Ground Truth
Method

 Highest confidence
reference for terrain
validation

 Operator can intentionally
place shots on bare-earth
patches, even under canopy

 Avoids ambiguity from

automated ground
classification

Validating Geometric Quality of 3DEP Lidar Data in Complex Terrain
17
17

TLS: Highly Accurate, but Classification-Limited

• Low bias and low RMSE when terrain is well resolved
• Dense sampling captures micro-topography well
• Limitation:

 Ground classification is the dominant error source
 Low-lying vegetation often retained as “ground”

• Conclusion: Data accuracy is high, but post-processing
and terrain-aware filtering are essential for validation
use.

Validating Geometric Quality of 3DEP Lidar Data in Complex Terrain
18
18

UAS Lidar: Scalable and Accurate When Properly
Controlled
• Covers large areas efficiently
• Performs well when:

 Independent control is available
 Calibration is performed

• Performance depends on:

 Calibration quality
 Ground filtering strategy

• Conclusion: UAS LiDAR is operationally powerful, but
like TLS, must rely on post-processed, terrain-aware
ground extraction for accurate validation.

Validating Geometric Quality of 3DEP Lidar Data in Complex Terrain
19
19

Overall Takeaways
•

•

Total Station based surveys define the best representation of
a single ground point.
TLS and UAS approach total station accuracy only after
careful post-processing.
Total Station: Best for accuracy assessment
TLS: Best for local detail, needs filtering

•
•
• UAS: Best for coverage, needs calibration + control
• Ground-classified ≠ ground truth

→ Terrain-aware, surface-based validation is required.

Validating Geometric Quality of 3DEP Lidar Data in Complex Terrain
20
20

Special Thanks

• Cindy Thatcher and Jason

Stoker - USGS NGP
• Travis Kropuenske –

EROS (KBR)

• Minsu Kim – EROS (KBR)
• Victoria Scholl – USGS

NUSO

• Drew Lane – USDA FPAC

Validating Geometric Quality of 3DEP Lidar Data in Complex Terrain
21

operationally difficult

•

 Yet accuracy under these conditions is critical for

downstream use
Validation Challenge
 Dense canopy and understory obscure true ground
 Ground classifiers (especially TLS and UAS) may retain

understory as ground

 Simple minimum-Z or point-to-point checks are insufficient

Validating Geometric Quality of 3DEP Lidar Data in Complex Terrain
2
2

Probably not the best way
to measure VVA.  Photo
from a VVA checkpoint from
a 3DEP collection over
Puerto Rico.

Background

 USGS and USDA
FPAC funded two
pilots to coincidentally
collect QL1 lidar and
NAIP imagery in CO
and WA in 2023

 USGS EROS collected
ground validation data
in urban and
vegetated
vegetated areas within
both pilot AOIs

Validating Geometric Quality of 3DEP Lidar Data in Complex Terrain

3

Reference / Independent Measurements

•

Instruments

 Total Station (Trimble SX10) (CO, WA)
 GNSS (Trimble R10-2 base and

rovers)(open skies, grass, low vegetation)
(CO)

 TLS (Riegl VZ-400i) (CO, WA)
 UAS Lidar (YellowScan Vx-20 (Riegl

miniVUX-1UAV + Applanix APX-20 UAV))
(CO)
Key Assumption: Terrain is complex,
often under canopy, where validation is
hardest but most meaningful

Note: Any use of trade, firm, or product names is for descriptive
purposes only and does not imply endorsement by the U.S.
Government.

•

•

Validating Geometric Quality of 3DEP Lidar Data in Complex Terrain
4
4

Validation Philosophy
•

Primary Question: How well does 3DEP Lidar model
topography under complex terrain and canopy?
Validation Approach: Compare 3DEP surfaces against:
 Ground truth (Total Station, GNSS where applicable)
 Independent Lidar modalities (TLS, UAS)

•

• Guiding Principle

 Model the local terrain geometry before computing error

Another example from
Puerto Rico.

•

This reduces bias from:
 Noise
 Density variation
 Small spatial misalignments

Validating Geometric Quality of 3DEP Lidar Data in Complex Terrain
5
5

Modeling the Local Terrain Geometry
•

Plane-based surface estimation (PCA), not minimum-Z selection
Percentile filtering improves robustness (bottom 10%)
Explicit geometric quality controls (planarity + verticality checks)

Objective: Extract a statistically robust, terrain slope aware lowest
ground envelope from classified Lidar.
Key Takeaways: Local modeling instead of global filtering (0.5 m
tiles)
–
–
–
Big Idea: Ground filters, particularly on TLS data can leave
understory classified as ground.
Use a nonaggressive ground classifier and extract
validation candidate regions with a locally adaptive, plane-
constrained, percentile-based method that is statistically
stable and computationally scalable.

•

•

•

Validating Geometric Quality of 3DEP Lidar Data in Complex Terrain
6
6

Data Processing

•

•

•

Spatial Binning: Ground-class points divided into fixed XY
tiles (0.5 m resolution)
Incremental Tile Statistics: Streaming computation of per-
tile:
– Mean (μ)
– Covariance matrix (Σ)
PCA Surface Fitting
– Eigen-decomposition of Σ
– Smallest eigenvector → plane normal
– Quality thresholds: Planarity ratio (λ

/ Σλ < threshold), Minimum

vertical component (nᶻ > 0.3)

Validating Geometric Quality of 3DEP Lidar Data in Complex Terrain
7
7

₀

Data Processing

• Signed Distance Analysis: Compute point-to-plane

signed distances

• Percentile Thresholding: Per tile, retain bottom 10% of

negative distances
 Defines local lowest envelope

• Output: Filtered LAS representing statistically controlled

lowest ground surface

Validating Geometric Quality of 3DEP Lidar Data in Complex Terrain
8
8

Filtering the Validation Point Clouds

 Ground

Classified
Points

Validating Geometric Quality of 3DEP Lidar Data in Complex Terrain

9

 Unclassified
TLS Point
Cloud

 Filtered

Points for
Validation

Local Quadratic Surface Fitting for 3DEP vs TLS and UAS
Evaluation
 Objective: Estimate vertical error

between reference ground points and
test Lidar surfaces using local
quadratic surface modeling.

 Idea: Model the local terrain geometry

before computing error —
reduces bias from noise, density
variation, and small misalignments.
 Core Concept - Instead of direct
point-to-point comparison:
→ Fit a local quadratic surface in the
test (3DEP) dataset
→ Evaluate surface at the reference
point location
→ Compute vertical and 3D error
metrics

Validating Geometric Quality of 3DEP Lidar Data in Complex Terrain

10

• Surface Model is a Quadratic
surface: z = ax² + by² + cxy +
dx + ey + f

 Two-stage fitting:
• Eigen-based solution →
initial parameter estimate
• Nonlinear least squares
→ refined surface fit

• Error Metrics

 Z error → surface value
at evaluation point (0,0)

• Per-point records stored
with associated 3DEP file

Total Stations : Most Accurate Ground Truth
Method

 Highest confidence
reference for terrain
validation

 Operator can intentionally
place shots on bare-earth
patches, even under canopy

 Avoids ambiguity from

automated ground
classification

Validating Geometric Quality of 3DEP Lidar Data in Complex Terrain
17
17

TLS: Highly Accurate, but Classification-Limited

• Low bias and low RMSE when terrain is well resolved
• Dense sampling captures micro-topography well
• Limitation:

 Ground classification is the dominant error source
 Low-lying vegetation often retained as “ground”

• Conclusion: Data accuracy is high, but post-processing
and terrain-aware filtering are essential for validation
use.

Validating Geometric Quality of 3DEP Lidar Data in Complex Terrain
18
18

UAS Lidar: Scalable and Accurate When Properly
Controlled
• Covers large areas efficiently
• Performs well when:

 Independent control is available
 Calibration is performed

• Performance depends on:

 Calibration quality
 Ground filtering strategy

• Conclusion: UAS LiDAR is operationally powerful, but
like TLS, must rely on post-processed, terrain-aware
ground extraction for accurate validation.

Validating Geometric Quality of 3DEP Lidar Data in Complex Terrain
19
19

Overall Takeaways
•

•

Total Station based surveys define the best representation of
a single ground point.
TLS and UAS approach total station accuracy only after
careful post-processing.
Total Station: Best for accuracy assessment
TLS: Best for local detail, needs filtering

•
•
• UAS: Best for coverage, needs calibration + control
• Ground-classified ≠ ground truth

→ Terrain-aware, surface-based validation is required.

Validating Geometric Quality of 3DEP Lidar Data in Complex Terrain

• False confidence in calibration

• Localized vertical errors masked by

statistics

• Inability to distinguish sensor bias

from terrain effects

• Flying outside ideal conditions often creates:

• Data voids
• Striping or banding
• Increased manual classification effort

Lidar Data Evaluation Using Cloud,
Open Source Data and Software

Authors: Ajit Sampath, Jeff Irwin, Barry Miller, Jason Stoker
ASPRS GEO WEEK 2026, 2/18/2026

Scalable, Cloud-Native Validation of 3DEP Lidar Quality

•

•

•

•

Context
– U.S. Geological Survey’s 3D Elevation Program (3DEP) has rapidly expanded national Lidar coverage
– Scale and diversity of terrain demand reproducible, validation methods
Approach
– Developed a cloud-native, open-source workflow for geometric accuracy and consistency assessment
– Built on PDAL, Open3D, and Amazon Web Services
– Integrates GNSS-surveyed ground control points and terrestrial laser scanning (TLS) as independent reference

data
Case Studies
– Puerto Rico: Detected vertical bias and spatial inconsistency in vegetated terrain
– Nationwide Ground Checkpoints (USGS)
– OSM Building footprints based analyses
Impact
– Enables efficient, repeatable national-scale validation of 3DEP Lidar
– Demonstrates that cloud + open-source tooling can operationalize large-scale geospatial QA
– Supports more reliable downstream decision-making in mapping, infrastructure, and environmental analysis

2

Data and Methods Overview

• Puerto Rico 3DEP LiDAR

• Two temporally separated collections (PR 2016–2017 and PR 2018)
• Cloud-hosted, accessed in web-Mercator projection

• Nationwide Ground Checkpoints (USGS)

• Consolidated survey-grade GCPs (NVA & VVA)
• Used for point-based vertical consistency assessment

• OpenStreetMap (OSM) Building Polygons

• Used to identify and localize built structures within user-defined AOIsNot used as a

height or truth reference

3

Surface Consistency (Puerto Rico)
• Overlapping regions identified between epochs
• Context-aware sampling of analysis locations
• Local LiDAR neighborhoods analyzed to compute:

Elevation

•
• Slope
• Surface roughness (eigenvalue-based)
• Point density (Voronoi-based)

• Metric differences used to assess multi-temporal

4

Checkpoint-Based Vertical Accuracy

• Local LiDAR neighborhoods streamed around

nationwide GNSS checkpoints

• Ground points used to fit local planar surfaces
• Signed vertical offsets computed (checkpoint → LiDAR
surface)Statistics reported by terrain class (NVA vs VVA)

5

Building-Based Relative Consistency
• User-defined AOIs within Denver (any region with

overlapping point cloud)

• Building footprints intersecting AOIs retrieved from OSM
• Random subset of buildings selected
•

LiDAR points extracted per building footprint and
structurally filtered

• Bidirectional point-to-plane ICP applied between datasets
• Mean per-axis displacements (ΔX, ΔY, ΔZ) reported per

building

6

Tools and Technologies

• This study utilizes cloud computing (AWS) and open-

source tools like Entwine Point Tile (EPT) and the Point
Data Abstraction Library (PDAL).

• These tools enable efficient processing and analysis of

large-scale 3DEP datasets.

• Leveraging these technologies allows for a more robust

and scalable approach to data quality assessment.

7

sampling

Data/Surface Consistency
•

•

The first case study compares two 3DEP datasets collected
over Puerto Rico at different times (2016/2017 and 2018).
This comparison aims to evaluate the consistency of 3DEP
data over time, which is crucial for long-term studies and
monitoring.

• Understanding data consistency helps ensure the reliability
of analyses that rely on 3DEP data collected across different
periods.

• We'll detail the specific methods used to assess data

consistency in this case study.

10

Workflow for Selecting Analysis Locations
and Computing Surface Consistency

•

•

•

Data and Surface consistency
was evaluated using point
density, elevation, slope, and
surface roughness.
Analysis sites were chosen
based on the National Land
Cover Database (NLCD).
Density, Elevation, Slope and
Surface Roughness offer a
thorough assessment of
consistency throughout
various elements of the
elevation dataset.

12

Defining the Analysis Region

•

•

•

Derive spatial
boundaries using alpha
shapes
Identify overlapping
coverage between
acquisitions using
geometric intersection
of the two alpha-shape
boundaries
Restricted analysis to
common spatial extent

13

Same Surface Precision

• Select planar, hard-surface locations

(Developed / NLCD Class 22)

• Extract local neighborhoods of ground-

classified Lidar points

• Fit a best-fit plane to each

neighborhood

• Compute RMSE of point-to-plane

residuals as a proxy for measurement
precision

• Hard surfaces approximate true planar

geometry

• Plane-fit RMSE provides a robust, data-
driven estimate of Lidar precision using
only the point cloud itself.

14

Point Density

• Constructed Voronoi polygons around

individual Lidar points

• Partitioned space so each polygon

represents the area closest to a given
point

• Computed local point density from

polygon areas

• Estimated density as the inverse of

polygon area

• Density = 1 / Area(Voronoi polygon)
• Restricted analysis to ground-

classified points

• Used single-return ground points to

reflect terrain-relevant density

• Aligned with primary 3DEP application

needs

• Aggregated density statistics across

analysis locations

15

Calculating Elevation and Slope Differences

•

•

•

•

•

•

For each analysis location, ground classified Lidar points within a 4-meter neighborhood and fit
with a 2D surface.

Developed surfaces show high geometric
consistency across epochs
• NLCD Class 22 – Developed (Low Intensity)

• Elevation differences are small and centered near zero
• Slope differences are low and stable
• Surface roughness differences are near zero
• Point density ratios are consistent across datasets
• Variability primarily reflects LiDAR system and processing consistency

• NLCD Class 71 – Grassland/Herbaceous
• Elevation differences are slightly larger and more dispersed
• Slope differences show greater variability
• Surface roughness is consistently higher
• Point density ratios are higher across acquisitions
• Variability reflects surface interaction in addition to system effects

19

Puerto Rico: Overall Findings

• Overall geometric consistency is high
• Point density increased substantially in later acquisition

• Implications for temporal analyses

• Vertical differences are low and spatially coherent
• Terrain type matters

• Greater variability observed in vegetated areas compared to developed surfaces
• Highlights importance of land-cover–aware analysis using National Land Cover Database

classes

• Method scales cleanly and is automatable

20

Case Study II: Data Accuracy - Focus

• The second case study uses field data (GNSS, total

station, terrestrial laser scanner) to assess the vertical
and horizontal accuracy of 3DEP data.

• Comparing 3DEP data with precise field measurements
helps determine how accurately 3DEP data represents
real-world conditions.

21

Checkpoint-Based Vertical Accuracy

• Local surface modeling at

checkpoints:

• Signed vertical error

computation

• Statistical aggregation by
surface class (NVA and
VVA where applicable) and
confirmed using NLCD API

22

Checkpoint Dataset

•

•

•

•

•

•

•

Dataset: Consolidated Standardized Survey-
Grade Checkpoints 3DEP 2004–2025 (ver. 2.0)

National inventory of GNSS survey checkpoints
used for Lidar accuracy assessment.

Includes 145,299 survey-grade points from 551
Lidar projects covering 1,874 published work
units.

Standardized and consolidated by the USGS
National Geospatial Technical Operations Center
(NGTOC) from disparate original delivery formats
(CSV, XML, PDF, shapefiles, etc.).

Points are classified as either:

• VVA: Vegetated Vertical Accuracy checkpoints
• NVA: Non-Vegetated Vertical Accuracy checkpoints

Checkpoints are linked back to their source Lidar
project URLs.

Purpose: support standardized assessment of
3DEP Lidar absolute vertical accuracy per
ASPRS standards.

23

How Checkpoints Were Used in Our Workflow

•

Spatial Filtering: only checkpoints collected since a user-defined
year were retained (e.g., ≥ 2017).

• Attribute Filtering: retained only "NVA" and "VVA" point types.
•

Sampling: random sampling of up to N checkpoints used as local
analysis seeds.

• Neighborhood Analysis: for each sampled checkpoint, we

•

•

streamed nearby Lidar from the associated 3DEP EPT repository.
Local Plane Estimation: fitted local planar surface through Lidar
points near each checkpoint.
Signed Vertical Offset: computed the signed vertical distance from
the checkpoint elevation to the Lidar local plane.

24

Post-2017 NVA data are more consistent

• Dispersion is clearly reduced

•

•

post-2017
• Std dev drops from ~10 cm → ~7 cm
Tighter interquartile range
• Shorter tails at the 95th percentile
Extremes still exist, but are
less frequent and less severe
• Bottom line: Later datasets are
not “more accurate” in mean
terms, but they are more
uniform and predictable.

26

Building-Based Lidar Consistency Analysis Workflow

• User-driven AOI → Buildings → ICP alignment
• User-Defined Area of Interest (AOI)
• The analysis begins with a user-provided AOI polygon.
• The AOI defines the full spatial extent of the building-based analysis and serves as the

sole geographic constraint.

27

Building Footprint
Selection (OpenStreetMap)

• Building footprints intersecting the AOI
are retrieved from OpenStreetMap
(OSM).

• OSM is used strictly for feature

localization (i.e., identifying building
locations and extents), not as a vertical
or geometric truth reference.

• Footprints are filtered to retain valid

Polygon geometries only.

28

Random Building Sampling

• A random subset of buildings within the AOI is selected

for analysis.

• Lidar Extraction per Building

• For each selected building footprint, Lidar points from all overlapping 3DEP datasets are

extracted.

• Points are spatially constrained to the building footprint (with a buffer) to isolate

building-related returns.

29

Example building

30

Structural Filtering and ICP-Based Alignment Analysis

• Structural Filtering

• Extracted Lidar points are filtered using local geometric criteria (e.g., normals and

eigenvalue-based planarity).

•

• Unstructured points such as vegetation, edges, and clutter are removed, retaining

predominantly planar building surfaces.
ICP Alignment and Displacement Analysis
• Rigid point-to-plane Iterative Closest Point (ICP) alignment is performed between

overlapping Lidar datasets for each building.

• Bidirectional ICP (forward and reverse) is used to assess alignment symmetry and

stability.

• Per-axis displacement statistics (ΔX, ΔY, ΔZ; median and RMSE) are computed for each

building.

31

Outcome

• Relative geometric consistency between LiDAR datasets

is quantified using real built structures

Summary and Key Insights
• Cloud + Open Source = Scalable Data Validation
• Case Studies Confirm Feasibility
• Puerto Rico: Detected temporal inconsistencies;

• Land Cover Matters
• Developed surfaces show high consistency; vegetated areas exhibit greater variability.
• Checkpoint AnalysisPost-2017 datasets more uniform;

minimal vertical bias in non-vegetated terrain.

• Building-Based Analysis

• ICP alignment demonstrates robust relative consistency across epochs

Scalable, Cloud-Native Validation of 3DEP Lidar Quality

•

•

•

•

Context
– U.S. Geological Survey’s 3D Elevation Program (3DEP) has rapidly expanded national Lidar coverage
– Scale and diversity of terrain demand reproducible, validation methods
Approach
– Developed a cloud-native, open-source workflow for geometric accuracy and consistency assessment
– Built on PDAL, Open3D, and Amazon Web Services
– Integrates GNSS-surveyed ground control points and terrestrial laser scanning (TLS) as independent reference

data
Case Studies
– Puerto Rico: Detected vertical bias and spatial inconsistency in vegetated terrain
– Nationwide Ground Checkpoints (USGS)
– OSM Building footprints based analyses
Impact
– Enables efficient, repeatable national-scale validation of 3DEP Lidar
– Demonstrates that cloud + open-source tooling can operationalize large-scale geospatial QA
– Supports more reliable downstream decision-making in mapping, infrastructure, and environmental analysis

2

Data and Methods Overview

• Puerto Rico 3DEP LiDAR

• Two temporally separated collections (PR 2016–2017 and PR 2018)
• Cloud-hosted, accessed in web-Mercator projection

• Nationwide Ground Checkpoints (USGS)

• Consolidated survey-grade GCPs (NVA & VVA)
• Used for point-based vertical consistency assessment

• OpenStreetMap (OSM) Building Polygons

• Used to identify and localize built structures within user-defined AOIsNot used as a

height or truth reference

3

Surface Consistency (Puerto Rico)
• Overlapping regions identified between epochs
• Context-aware sampling of analysis locations
• Local LiDAR neighborhoods analyzed to compute:

Elevation

•
• Slope
• Surface roughness (eigenvalue-based)
• Point density (Voronoi-based)

• Metric differences used to assess multi-temporal

4

Checkpoint-Based Vertical Accuracy

• Local LiDAR neighborhoods streamed around

nationwide GNSS checkpoints

• Ground points used to fit local planar surfaces
• Signed vertical offsets computed (checkpoint → LiDAR
surface)Statistics reported by terrain class (NVA vs VVA)

5

Building-Based Relative Consistency
• User-defined AOIs within Denver (any region with

overlapping point cloud)

• Building footprints intersecting AOIs retrieved from OSM
• Random subset of buildings selected
•

LiDAR points extracted per building footprint and
structurally filtered

• Bidirectional point-to-plane ICP applied between datasets
• Mean per-axis displacements (ΔX, ΔY, ΔZ) reported per

building

6

• This study utilizes cloud computing (AWS) and open-

source tools like Entwine Point Tile (EPT) and the Point
Data Abstraction Library (PDAL).

• These tools enable efficient processing and analysis of

large-scale 3DEP datasets.

• Leveraging these technologies allows for a more robust

and scalable approach to data quality assessment.

Developed surfaces show high geometric
consistency across epochs
• NLCD Class 22 – Developed (Low Intensity)

• Elevation differences are small and centered near zero
• Slope differences are low and stable
• Surface roughness differences are near zero
• Point density ratios are consistent across datasets
• Variability primarily reflects LiDAR system and processing consistency

• NLCD Class 71 – Grassland/Herbaceous
• Elevation differences are slightly larger and more dispersed
• Slope differences show greater variability
• Surface roughness is consistently higher
• Point density ratios are higher across acquisitions
• Variability reflects surface interaction in addition to system effects

19

Puerto Rico: Overall Findings

• Overall geometric consistency is high
• Point density increased substantially in later acquisition

• Implications for temporal analyses

• Vertical differences are low and spatially coherent
• Terrain type matters

• Greater variability observed in vegetated areas compared to developed surfaces
• Highlights importance of land-cover–aware analysis using National Land Cover Database

classes

• Method scales cleanly and is automatable

20

Case Study II: Data Accuracy - Focus

• The second case study uses field data (GNSS, total

station, terrestrial laser scanner) to assess the vertical
and horizontal accuracy of 3DEP data.

• Comparing 3DEP data with precise field measurements
helps determine how accurately 3DEP data represents
real-world conditions.

21

Checkpoint-Based Vertical Accuracy

• Local surface modeling at

checkpoints:

• Signed vertical error

computation

• Statistical aggregation by
surface class (NVA and
VVA where applicable) and
confirmed using NLCD API

22

Checkpoint Dataset

•

•

•

•

•

•

•

Dataset: Consolidated Standardized Survey-
Grade Checkpoints 3DEP 2004–2025 (ver. 2.0)

National inventory of GNSS survey checkpoints
used for Lidar accuracy assessment.

Includes 145,299 survey-grade points from 551
Lidar projects covering 1,874 published work
units.

Standardized and consolidated by the USGS
National Geospatial Technical Operations Center
(NGTOC) from disparate original delivery formats
(CSV, XML, PDF, shapefiles, etc.).

Points are classified as either:

• VVA: Vegetated Vertical Accuracy checkpoints
• NVA: Non-Vegetated Vertical Accuracy checkpoints

Checkpoints are linked back to their source Lidar
project URLs.

Purpose: support standardized assessment of
3DEP Lidar absolute vertical accuracy per
ASPRS standards.

23

How Checkpoints Were Used in Our Workflow

•

Spatial Filtering: only checkpoints collected since a user-defined
year were retained (e.g., ≥ 2017).

• Attribute Filtering: retained only "NVA" and "VVA" point types.
•

Sampling: random sampling of up to N checkpoints used as local
analysis seeds.

• Neighborhood Analysis: for each sampled checkpoint, we

•

•

streamed nearby Lidar from the associated 3DEP EPT repository.
Local Plane Estimation: fitted local planar surface through Lidar
points near each checkpoint.
Signed Vertical Offset: computed the signed vertical distance from
the checkpoint elevation to the Lidar local plane.

24

• Bottom line: Later datasets are
not “more accurate” in mean

uniform and predictable.
