Building-Based Lidar Consistency Analysis Workflow

• User-driven AOI → Buildings → ICP alignment
• User-Defined Area of Interest (AOI)
• The analysis begins with a user-provided AOI polygon.
• The AOI defines the full spatial extent of the building-based analysis and serves as the

sole geographic constraint.

27

Building Footprint
Selection (OpenStreetMap)

• Building footprints intersecting the AOI
are retrieved from OpenStreetMap
(OSM).

• OSM is used strictly for feature

localization (i.e., identifying building
locations and extents), not as a vertical
or geometric truth reference.

• Footprints are filtered to retain valid

Polygon geometries only.

28

Random Building Sampling

• A random subset of buildings within the AOI is selected

for analysis.

• Lidar Extraction per Building

• For each selected building footprint, Lidar points from all overlapping 3DEP datasets are

extracted.

• Points are spatially constrained to the building footprint (with a buffer) to isolate

building-related returns.

29

Example building

30

Structural Filtering and ICP-Based Alignment Analysis

• Structural Filtering

• Extracted Lidar points are filtered using local geometric criteria (e.g., normals and

eigenvalue-based planarity).

•

• Unstructured points such as vegetation, edges, and clutter are removed, retaining

predominantly planar building surfaces.
ICP Alignment and Displacement Analysis
• Rigid point-to-plane Iterative Closest Point (ICP) alignment is performed between

overlapping Lidar datasets for each building.

• Bidirectional ICP (forward and reverse) is used to assess alignment symmetry and

stability.

• Per-axis displacement statistics (ΔX, ΔY, ΔZ; median and RMSE) are computed for each

building.

31

Outcome

• Relative geometric consistency between LiDAR datasets

is quantified using real built structures

• Cloud + Open Source = Scalable Data Validation
• Case Studies Confirm Feasibility
• Puerto Rico: Detected temporal inconsistencies;

• Land Cover Matters
• Developed surfaces show high consistency; vegetated areas exhibit greater variability.
• Checkpoint AnalysisPost-2017 datasets more uniform;

minimal vertical bias in non-vegetated terrain.

• ICP alignment demonstrates robust relative consistency across epochs

Developed surfaces show high geometric
consistency across epochs
• NLCD Class 22 – Developed (Low Intensity)

• Elevation differences are small and centered near zero
• Slope differences are low and stable
• Surface roughness differences are near zero
• Point density ratios are consistent across datasets
• Variability primarily reflects LiDAR system and processing consistency

• NLCD Class 71 – Grassland/Herbaceous
• Elevation differences are slightly larger and more dispersed
• Slope differences show greater variability
• Surface roughness is consistently higher
• Point density ratios are higher across acquisitions
• Variability reflects surface interaction in addition to system effects

19

Puerto Rico: Overall Findings

• Overall geometric consistency is high
• Point density increased substantially in later acquisition

• Implications for temporal analyses

• Vertical differences are low and spatially coherent
• Terrain type matters

• Greater variability observed in vegetated areas compared to developed surfaces
• Highlights importance of land-cover–aware analysis using National Land Cover Database

classes

• Method scales cleanly and is automatable

20

Case Study II: Data Accuracy - Focus

• The second case study uses field data (GNSS, total

station, terrestrial laser scanner) to assess the vertical
and horizontal accuracy of 3DEP data.

• Comparing 3DEP data with precise field measurements
helps determine how accurately 3DEP data represents
real-world conditions.

21

Checkpoint-Based Vertical Accuracy

• Local surface modeling at

checkpoints:

• Signed vertical error

computation

• Statistical aggregation by
surface class (NVA and
VVA where applicable) and
confirmed using NLCD API

22

Checkpoint Dataset

•

•

•

•

•

•

•

Dataset: Consolidated Standardized Survey-
Grade Checkpoints 3DEP 2004–2025 (ver. 2.0)

National inventory of GNSS survey checkpoints
used for Lidar accuracy assessment.

Includes 145,299 survey-grade points from 551
Lidar projects covering 1,874 published work
units.

Standardized and consolidated by the USGS
National Geospatial Technical Operations Center
(NGTOC) from disparate original delivery formats
(CSV, XML, PDF, shapefiles, etc.).

Points are classified as either:

• VVA: Vegetated Vertical Accuracy checkpoints
• NVA: Non-Vegetated Vertical Accuracy checkpoints

Checkpoints are linked back to their source Lidar
project URLs.

Purpose: support standardized assessment of
3DEP Lidar absolute vertical accuracy per
ASPRS standards.

23

How Checkpoints Were Used in Our Workflow

•

Spatial Filtering: only checkpoints collected since a user-defined
year were retained (e.g., ≥ 2017).

• Attribute Filtering: retained only "NVA" and "VVA" point types.
•

Sampling: random sampling of up to N checkpoints used as local
analysis seeds.

• Neighborhood Analysis: for each sampled checkpoint, we

•

•

streamed nearby Lidar from the associated 3DEP EPT repository.
Local Plane Estimation: fitted local planar surface through Lidar
points near each checkpoint.
Signed Vertical Offset: computed the signed vertical distance from
the checkpoint elevation to the Lidar local plane.

24

• Dispersion is clearly reduced

•

•

post-2017

Tighter interquartile range
• Shorter tails at the 95th percentile
Extremes still exist, but are
less frequent and less severe
• Bottom line: Later datasets are
not “more accurate” in mean
terms, but they are more
uniform and predictable.

Building-Based Lidar Consistency Analysis Workflow

• User-driven AOI → Buildings → ICP alignment
• User-Defined Area of Interest (AOI)
• The analysis begins with a user-provided AOI polygon.
• The AOI defines the full spatial extent of the building-based analysis and serves as the

sole geographic constraint.

27

Building Footprint
Selection (OpenStreetMap)

• Building footprints intersecting the AOI
are retrieved from OpenStreetMap
(OSM).

• OSM is used strictly for feature

localization (i.e., identifying building
locations and extents), not as a vertical
or geometric truth reference.

• Footprints are filtered to retain valid

Polygon geometries only.

28

Random Building Sampling

• A random subset of buildings within the AOI is selected

for analysis.

• Lidar Extraction per Building

• For each selected building footprint, Lidar points from all overlapping 3DEP datasets are

extracted.

• Points are spatially constrained to the building footprint (with a buffer) to isolate

building-related returns.

29

Example building

30

Structural Filtering and ICP-Based Alignment Analysis

• Structural Filtering

• Extracted Lidar points are filtered using local geometric criteria (e.g., normals and

eigenvalue-based planarity).

•

• Unstructured points such as vegetation, edges, and clutter are removed, retaining

predominantly planar building surfaces.
ICP Alignment and Displacement Analysis
• Rigid point-to-plane Iterative Closest Point (ICP) alignment is performed between

overlapping Lidar datasets for each building.

• Bidirectional ICP (forward and reverse) is used to assess alignment symmetry and

stability.

• Per-axis displacement statistics (ΔX, ΔY, ΔZ; median and RMSE) are computed for each

building.

31

Outcome

• Relative geometric consistency between LiDAR datasets

is quantified using real built structures

Summary and Key Insights
• Cloud + Open Source = Scalable Data Validation
• Case Studies Confirm Feasibility
• Puerto Rico: Detected temporal inconsistencies;

• Land Cover Matters
• Developed surfaces show high consistency; vegetated areas exhibit greater variability.
• Checkpoint AnalysisPost-2017 datasets more uniform;

minimal vertical bias in non-vegetated terrain.

name: Dharm Barot
markdown:
Integrated Geospatial Analysis of Burn
Severity and Vegetation Recovery of the
California August Complex Fire in 2020

Dharm Barot, Ronny Schroeder, Elise Anderson

Embry-Riddle Aeronautical University

Outline

Background &
Study Area

Importance of
Wildfires

Research Goal
& Hypothesis

Methodology

Results

Conclusions

Background &
Study Area

• August Complex Fire occurred in
August 2020 in Mendocino National
Forest, California.
• Started by 38 lightning strikes.
• Became the largest wildfire in
California’s history at the time by
burning more than 1 million acres.

Importance of Wildfires

• Wildfires are increasing in size and intensity.

• Wildfires affect ecosystem functioning, water supply, biodiversity, and

future fire risk.

• Unlike ground-based surveys, observations from space lets us measure

wildfire damage and regrowth at landscape scales.

Research Goal & Hypothesis

Goal :- Measure and detect burn scars and assess
vegetation recovery in the August Complex Fire using
satellite imagery and airborne LiDAR observations.

Hypothesis :-
• 5 years following the August Complex Fire

vegetation has recovered completely

• Vegetation recovery rates depends on the

vegetation type, forests recovery slower than
grasslands

Normalized Burn Ratio (NBR)

• Highlights burned vs. healthy vegetation

• Uses NIR and SWIR bands:

Formula: (NIR − SWIR) / (NIR + SWIR)

Healthy vegetation: High NIR / low SWIR  positive NBR

Burned areas: Low NIR / high SWIR  negative NBR

• Used to measure burn severity and vegetation loss

Methodology

Data Sets

Satellite Landsat @ 30m/pixel, multispectral (2015, 2020, 2025 from
Earthexplorer.gov)

Airborne USGS LiDAR (2018, National Map Viewer, 3DEP)

Processing

Manually Digitize burn scar to isolate affected areas

Calculated NBR to assess burn severity

Sample LiDAR tile of vegetation classification to measure vegetation height.

Analysis

NBR change detection to assess vegetation recovery

Hotspot Analysis to detect sever burn scars

Compute recovery rates using average NBR for each year (2015, 2020, 2025)

Results (1/5)

NBR Timeline
 2015 - 2020 -2025

•

  2015 — pre-fire vegetation
(high NBR values)
• 2020 — fire event
     (low NBR values)
• 2025 — post-fire
  vegetation recovery
Standardized min/max stretch
applied for fair comparison

Results (2/5)

Detection of Burn Scars
Using Difference Map

• Orange and red areas represent

burn activity

• Very red locations signify greatest

burn severity

• Green and blue areas represent

unaffected areas

Results (3/5)

Assessment of Post Fire
Vegetation Recovery

• Areal summary statistics

(mean NBR) extracted for
burn scar polygon

• Line chart shows vegetation

loss (2015 → 2020) and
recovery trend (2020 → 2025)

• 2025 imagery shows ~85%
recovery compared to 2015
• Recovery rate is biased by

areas not affected by the fire

Accurate Detection of Burn
Scars Using Optimized
Hotspot Analyses

Results (4/5)

• Hotspots identify statistically
significant clusters of burn
activity

• Coldspot identify areas not

affected by the fire

• Burn activity is concentrated in
the eastern & northeastern
region

• Removes bias and improves

vegetation recovery assessment

Results (5/5)

Vegetation Hight Classes:

Assessment of Vegetation
Cover & Height using
LiDAR Data

• Low vegetation = light
green (Ground to 2m)
• Medium vegetation =
green ( 2 to 5 m)

• High vegetation = dark

green (5 to 50 m)
• Helps identify forest,

shrubland and grassland

Conclusions (1/2)

• NBR difference map successfully identified burned areas

• Hotspot analysis refines the detection of burns scars and removes

bias from the calculation of vegetation recovery rates

• LiDAR helped to identify the landcover affected by the fire (e.g.,

forest, shrubland and grassland) supporting understanding of

vegetation recovery rates

Conclusions ( 2/2)

•

•

Integrated methods like the above provide a more complete post-fire

recovery assessment at landscape scales

 These insights supports forest management and hazard mitigation

planning by identifying areas requiring longer restoration time.

Sources

• Mendocino National Forest - About the Area. (2025, March 25). Forest

Service. https://www.fs.usda.gov/r05/mendocino/about-area

• Health, F. (2022, February 18). August Complex. ArcGIS StoryMaps; Esri.

https://storymaps.arcgis.com/stories/1a0a88e7872e48919831793fd496b814

Thank you!
summary: This study analyzed the burn severity and vegetation recovery after the California August Complex Fire in 2020 using satellite imagery and airborne LiDAR data, finding that while there was substantial recovery, it varied depending on vegetation type.

name: Devon Borthwick
markdown:
Variations in Volunteer Identified
Damage Types in Post Disaster
Aerial Photos

Presenter:
Devon Borthwick – Candidate M.S. GIST, University of Wyoming School of
Computing
Authors:
Devon Borthwick, Ramesh Sivanpillai, Jake Hawes, Jeff Hamerlinck, Tim
Robinson, University of Wyoming

Cognition and Labeling Objects

What about
this?

What is this?

What is this?

Is it half full
or half
empty?

All could be categorized as multiple objects based on
your perception and semantics.

How do people classify objects?

• Group objects based on our perception of objects based on our

environment (the context) and what we’ve seen before (Anderson,
1991)

• Objects can fit in multiple categories (Richler & Palmeri, 2013)

Problems with VGI for damage assessment

Variations in labeling based on
perceptions

Variations based on explanation
(semantics)

Structure Destroyed

Structure Displaced

So What?

• In a disaster situation, volunteers

can’t be screened and picked based
on experience and perception.

• Variation is going to happen

• To make Volunteer Generated

Information (VGI) useful, we need a
framework to follow.

• Best practices for using

volunteers

(NOAA, 2011)

What kind of variability occurs when
collecting data with volunteers with
various experience levels?

How much does it change under
different labeling schemes?

Why is this research important?

• What is the most efficient and accurate way to VGI after a disaster?

• Knowing the most effective way to record information can decrease the time it takes

to create products with the data
• Maps can become more accurate

Allows responders to get more accurate maps in less time, making
them more useful

Objectives

To evaluate whether the use of predefined response options leads to
greater accuracy and consistency in volunteer damage classifications

To compare the efficiency of typed versus predefined recording
methods

To identify recording approaches that minimize human error and
improve the reliability of disaster damage mapproducts.

Volunteer Mapping Project – Fall 2024 –
Hurricane Milton, Florida, USA

Borthwick, Geo Week 2025

Comments:
Describe any important
information / how sure are you of
the damage

Damage Type: Visual
Assessment of what is
damaged – early on varied
greatly

Borthwick, Geo Week 2025

Pre-Hurricane – ArcGIS
Imagery Basemap

Post-Hurricane – NOAA Aerial
Imagery

Borthwick, Geo Week 2025

Original Recorded Damage Types

Developed Damage Codes

Semantic variations

Borthwick, Geo Week 2025

Perceptual variations

Borthwick, Geo Week 2025

Materials & Methods

Image Selection

48 images from NOAA aerial imagery of structures
damaged by natural disasters – acquired from HDDS
Explorer clipped to structures extent.
• 6 example of each damage type * 8 damage

types = 48 images

• LA Wildfires (2025), Joplin Tornado (2011),

Hurricane Helene (2024), and Hurricane Milton
(2024).

• As images were selected, they were assigned a

damage type – later used a “key”.

• 8 damage types pulled from Fall 2024 project

• Adjusted for clarity using FEMA standards as a reference

(FEMA, 2025).

• Pre-event images came from ArcGIS Pro Imagery

basemap and HDDS Explorer.

Damage Type

Number of Images

Roof Damage

Roof Missing

Fence Damage

Structure Damage

Structure Destroyed

Structure Missing

Structure Displaced

Dock Damage

6

6

6

6

6

6

6

6

(NOAA, 2024)

Materials & Methods

Survey Design

Three different surveys – using the same images but asking participant to label the
damages in different ways.

1. Survey A - Blank Entry – provide damage type and comment (optional) in text box
2. Survey B - Guided Blank Entry– provide a list of possible damages and enter in text box
3. Survey C - Specific Damage Type Selection – select one from provide list (multiple

choice)

• 48 image-based questions – participants compare pre-event and post-event images to

decided damage that occurred to structure.

•  All survey versions asked participants their UW affiliation (student, staff, or faculty), and the

number of GIS or remote sensing courses they have taken (1-2, 3-4, 5 or more).

Materials & Methods

Survey Sessions

• Participants signed up for a time slot

using a Doodle poll.

• Once they arrived, participants were

provided with a link to one of the three
surveys and if applicable a reference
sheet with damage types.

• Participants received basic instructions

on how to identify damages, then worked
independently.

• 53 participants over 16 days of survey

sessions over 8 weeks.
• Survey A – 17 respondents
• Survey B – 18 respondents
• Survey C – 18 respondents

Materials & Methods

Analysis

• Descriptive statistics for accuracy and efficiency
• Accuracy: How “correct” were the participants?
• Efficiency: How long did it take them to complete the survey?

• Word for word accuracy

• Did the participants responses match exactly with the key?

• Interpreted accuracy - Survey A & B Only

• How similar conceptually are the responses?

• Kruskal-Wallis Test

• Followed up with Dunn’s post-hoc test.
• Determine if there is a significant difference between any of the surveys for accuracy and

efficiency.
• Qualitative Analysis

Results

Accuracy and Efficiency

• Accuracy goes up for Survey A

and B when interpreted
• Very large amount of

variation in accuracy of
Survey A

• Survey B and C are generally
more consistent in accuracy

• Survey C took the least amount

of time

Results

Accuracy

Plots show no relationship between accuracy and
the number of GIS or Remote Sensing courses
taken.

Results

Efficiency

Plots show little to
no relationship
between efficiency
and the number of
GIS or Remote
Sensing courses
taken.

Results

Kruskal-Wallis & Dunn’s

• Significant difference found

between surveys

• Accuracy – Between A and B, and

A and C

• Survey w/o reference vs.
Surveys w/ reference
• Efficiency (Time Taken) –

Between A and C, and B and C

• Typed responses vs. Multiple

choice responses

Results

Qualitative Analysis of Survey A & B

Survey A
• Long descriptive phrases

• “docks and structures were destroyed

and pushed into the water”

• Repeated adjectives

• Extreme, Moderate, Partially, Fully

• Repeated damage types

• Coming up with their own list of

damage types

• Described the potential causes of

damages

• Tornado, Flood, Wind

Survey B
• Selection of more than one damage

type

• “structure missing and fence damage”

• Typos
• Used damage type from list in longer

description

• “Structure missing and roof damage to

smaller structures”

Discussion

Recommendations – Labeling Schemes

• Using a multiple-choice format, you lose volunteer’s creativity but

gain consistency and speed (Lin et al., 2015)

• Describe all of the damage in the images
• Multiple damage types
• Must know what damage types to include
• Typed responses take time to interpret

• Survey A: ~70 minutes
• Survey B: ~45 minutes

Typed responses with list of recommended damage types allow
for creativity while keeping consistency (Survey B)

Discussion

Recommendations – Other considerations

• Training for volunteers – teach them how to categorize damages
• Can impact the accuracy of categorizations (Richler & Palmeri, 2013)

(Barrington et al., 2011)

• Takes more time

• UI design and cognitive load

• Keeping the method of entering damage types simple can potentially

increase accuracy and productivity (Rahmanian & Davis, 2014)

• Picking a labeling scheme that is more straightforward and providing

descriptions of damage types.

Acknowledgments

NOAA for high resolution aerial imagery.
USGS for hosting data for imagery download.
ESRI for pre-event imagery from ArcGIS Pro.
University of Wyoming Research and Economic Development
Division for IRB approval.
53 volunteer participants for their time and energy.
Wyoming View for 2025 summer funding.
School of Computing for funding support.

References

Anderson, J. R. (1991). The adaptive nature of human categorization. In Psychological
Review (Vol. 98, Issue 3, pp. 409–429).
Bahareh Rahmanian, & Davis, J. G. (2014). User interface design for crowdsourcing
systems. 405–408. https://doi.org/10.1145/2598153.2602248
Barrington, L., Ghosh, S., Greene, M., Har-Noy, S., Berger, J., Gill, S., Yu, A., & Huyck, C.
(2011). Crowdsourcing earthquake damage assessment using remote sensing
imagery. Annals of Geophysics, 54(6), 680–687. https://doi.org/10.4401/ag-5324
FEMA. (2025). FEMA preliminary damage assessment guide.
Lin, Y. L., Trattner, C., Brusilovsky, P., & He, D. (2015). The impact of image descriptions
on user tagging behavior: A study of the nature and functionality of crowdsourced
tags. Journal of the Association for Information Science and Technology, 66(9), 1785–
1798. https://doi.org/10.1002/asi.23292
Richler, J. J., & Palmeri, T. J. (2014). Visual category learning. Wiley Interdisciplinary
Reviews: Cognitive Science, 5(1), 75–94. https://doi.org/10.1002/wcs.1268

Thank You!

Questions or Comments:
Devon Borthwick – dborthw1@uwyo.edu

Geo Week 2026

Nick Martz & Carl Schwarz

What is a Digital Twin

• A digital representation of assets, processes or systems
in the built or natural environment. What distinguishes a
digital twin from any other digital model or replica is its
connection to its physical twin.

ASSET

DIGITAL
TWIN

•

Its value is it efficiently delivers necessary data to
determine future condition of an asset or inform
operations.

Digital Twin Maturity
Matrix

• Phase 1: Digital Model (Static Foundation) –

A highly accurate, static 3D replica containing
geometry and metadata; it defines what the
building is.

• Phase 2: Digital Shadow (One-Way

Connection) – A dynamic link where the
physical building sends live sensor data (IoT)
to the model, allowing you to monitor real-
time performance.

• Phase 3: Digital Twin (Bi-Directional

Autonomy) – A fully integrated system where
AI predicts future issues and can
autonomously control building systems to
optimize operations.

Spatial/Graphical Representation

The Spatial/Graphical
Representation serves as the "Digital
Foundation" for all future intelligence. It
provides the essential geometry and
coordinate system that all other data
must interact with

Drone imagery, Data courtesy: Esri

Stuttgart: 4cm, altitude: ~3,300 ft (~1000m), 80/60, Vexcel Ultracam Osprey 4.1, courtesy of Geofly

3.98’ = 47.8”

~47.4”

Geospatial Gaussian Splats: Expectations

Far beyond a nice picture:

• Georeferenced

• Scalable

• Streamable

• Standardized

• Trustworthy

• Measurable

Geospatial Gaussian Splats: Accuracy

Gaussian splatting is stunning...

but is it accurate?

• Short answer: not in its initial form

• Gaussian splatting

- optimizes rendering fidelity from input viewpoints

- only yields a local minimum

- does not model/enforce 3D consistency/accuracy

 Prominent 3D inaccuracies, even on good data

Geospatial Gaussian Splats: Findings and approach

• Systematic 3D inaccuracies

- Floaters

- Shrinking bias

- Trompe l’œil

 Careful engineering of initialization, loss
function and training loop

- Questioning the widely accepted formulation

- to avoid obvious local minima

- to avoid systematic biases

- Using know-how in photogrammetry

- to accurately georeference

- to introduce prior knowledge about 3D scenes

Shrinking bias

• Cause: Image-space smoothing during training

 Shrinkage of up to ~2x GSD

• Our solution

- Eliminates the shrinking bias

- Preserves antialiasing / super-resolution

- Soon: contribution to open source

Shrinking bias

Shrinking bias

Trompe l’œil

• Cause: Unwanted coupling between splats color

and opacity
 Higher non-convexity of problem
 Abuse of multiple transparent layers

• Our solution

- Yields one solid surface (where sufficient)

- Simple modification of loss function

Trompe l’œil
summary: The research focuses on using Geospatial Gaussian Splats for autonomous asset monitoring by integrating reality capture, artificial intelligence, and geospatial innovation. It explores methods to improve accuracy and reliability while addressing issues like shrinking bias and trompe l'oeil effects.

name: Jon Miranda
markdown:
From Points to Projects: Unlocking the
Power of LiDAR Beyond Survey
Nathan Goins & Jonathan Miranda

Introduction and
Role of LiDAR in
Modern Projects

Evolution of LiDAR Technology
•

LiDAR evolved from a basic survey tool
to a core engine of project intelligence
in civil engineering.

From Surface Capture to
Actionable Intelligence
•

LiDAR transitioned from simple data
capture to delivering actionable insights
for design and construction decisions.

Driving Project Efficiency and
Innovation
•

Leveraging LiDAR as a core asset helps
reduce risks, improve efficiency, and
foster innovation in infrastructure
projects.

Mindset Shift: From
Data Capture to
Capability

Evolving LiDAR Perception
•

LiDAR is shifting from a simple survey
tool to a powerful engine for reality
modeling and project intelligence.

Focus on Capability
•

The key question evolves to 'What can
we do with captured data?', unlocking
advanced capabilities like automation
and AI detection.

Enhanced Collaboration and
Outcomes
•

Focusing on capability enables better
collaboration between survey and
design teams, improving workflows and
project results.

Structured
Models Enable
Model-Based
Decision Making

Evolution from Raw Point Clouds
Transitioning from overwhelming raw
•
point clouds to structured models
enhances data usability and reduces
processing strain.

Structured Models Components
Structured models include surfaces,
•
meshes, breaklines, and condition-
indexed geometry for richer spatial
context.

Actionable Model-Based Decisions
Structured models enable automated
•
analysis like crack detection, clearance
checks, and quantity extraction for
informed decisions.

Industry Workflow Advancement
•

The industry is moving toward using
structured models for improved
accuracy, efficiency, and collaboration.

Design Intent vs.
Constructed
Reality

Gap Between Design and Reality
• Construction often deviates from the
original design due to field conditions
and unforeseen obstacles.

Importance of Real-Time
Decisions
• On-site decisions lead to variations,
highlighting the dynamic nature of
construction projects.

Value of Digital As-Builts
• Digital as-builts provide accurate
records capturing the constructed
reality beyond design documents.

Layering Contextual Data
•

Imagery, meshes, CAD features, and
field photos are layered onto the LiDAR
skeleton to provide comprehensive
context.

Enabling
Downstream Uses
and Reducing
Uncertainty

Unified Spatial Backbone
• Digital as-builts create a single spatial

source supporting design, construction,
and operational needs seamlessly.

Data Fusion for Accuracy
• Combining LiDAR, imagery, and field

data reduces uncertainty and enriches
project context effectively.

Improved Decision-Making
• Managing projects with integrated

spatial data reduces risks, unexpected
redesigns, and supports better
decisions.

Accuracy,
Repeatability,
Automation, and
Safety

Accuracy in Design Verification
LiDAR provides consistent geometric
•
data that ensures precise design and
verification processes.

Repeatability for Change
Detection
• Repeated LiDAR captures allow for

detailed comparison and detection of
changes over time.

Automation Streamlines
Workflow
• Clean LiDAR inputs enable automated
checks, faster modeling, and more
efficient project workflows.

Enhanced Safety for Field Crews
• Digital LiDAR captures reduce fieldwork
near live traffic, lowering risk for crews.

Indiana Signal Pole Case Study

Practical Examples and Scalable Approaches

LiDAR Workflows

• Three practical workflows demonstrating

fusion of LiDAR with imagery and 3D
meshes for diverse projects.

Solutions at Scale

• Workflows designed to be scaled or
tailored to fit different client needs,
budgets, and project scopes.

Quality Control

• Emphasizes simple QA/QC methods

and the use of phone-based LiDAR for
efficient daily engineering capture.

Leaning Pole Identified at Signal Modernization Site

Field Observation

Light pole was clearly leaning

Engineering assumption

There was a foundation deterioration or localized material failure

Location

SR 18/SR 15 modernization in Grant County, Indiana

Action

A structural and subsurface investigation took place.

GPR Deployed: Initial
Subsurface Indicators

Objective:

400 MHz antenna (~5–6 ft depth).

Identify
underground
voids and
vaulted
basements

Out-of-phase reflections indicate
concrete air boundary.

Signatures suggested vaulted
basement beneath sidewalk.

Plan View: Vaulted Basement Under the Sidewalk

1. Intersection corner show vaulted basements.

2. Vault extends beneath sidewalk toward curb.

3. Soil support missing under pole location.

Terrestrial LiDAR Confirms Why the Pole Was
Leaning

LiDAR revealed
the 3D
geometry of
the basement
under roadway.

Basement wall
aligns directly
beneath pole.

The Real Cause: Basement Extended to the Roadway

Not a pole hardware or isolated
foundation issue.

Historic basement construction
extended under sidewalk to curb.

Pole footing ended up positioned
over void causing lean.

PREVENTING HEAD-ON COLLISIONS IN WORK
ZONES WITH MOBILE MAPPING

INDIANA CASE STUDY OF A FATAL COLLISION

Fatal Work Zone Collision
• A 2025 head-on collision near Kokomo, Indiana caused a
fatality involving a construction worker and two vehicles.

Contributing Roadway Factors
• Narrow lanes, compressed traffic, and abrupt lane

changes created hazardous conditions leading to the
collision.

Worker Vulnerability
• Road workers are exposed to high risk due to proximity
to active lanes without adequate protection barriers.

Safety Technology Importance
• Enhanced monitoring technologies are crucial for early
detection of geometric or operational risks in work
zones.

GROWING RISK ENVIRONMENT IN WORK ZONES
Increasing Crash Risks
• Work zones in the US show rising crash rates

and fatalities, reaching 898 deaths and
101,000 crashes in 2023.

Challenges of Lane Geometry
• Narrow lanes and shifting alignments in work
zones complicate driver maneuverability,
increasing collisions.

Insufficient Physical Barriers

markdown:
From Points to Projects: Unlocking the
Power of LiDAR Beyond Survey
Nathan Goins & Jonathan Miranda

Introduction and
Role of LiDAR in
Modern Projects

Evolution of LiDAR Technology
•

LiDAR evolved from a basic survey tool
to a core engine of project intelligence
in civil engineering.

From Surface Capture to
Actionable Intelligence
•

LiDAR transitioned from simple data
capture to delivering actionable insights
for design and construction decisions.

Driving Project Efficiency and
Innovation
•

Leveraging LiDAR as a core asset helps
reduce risks, improve efficiency, and
foster innovation in infrastructure
projects.

Mindset Shift: From
Data Capture to
Capability

Evolving LiDAR Perception
•

LiDAR is shifting from a simple survey
tool to a powerful engine for reality
modeling and project intelligence.

Focus on Capability
•

The key question evolves to 'What can
we do with captured data?', unlocking
advanced capabilities like automation
and AI detection.

Enhanced Collaboration and
Outcomes
•

Focusing on capability enables better
collaboration between survey and
design teams, improving workflows and
project results.

Structured
Models Enable
Model-Based
Decision Making

Evolution from Raw Point Clouds
Transitioning from overwhelming raw
•
point clouds to structured models
enhances data usability and reduces
processing strain.

Structured Models Components
Structured models include surfaces,
•
meshes, breaklines, and condition-
indexed geometry for richer spatial
context.

Actionable Model-Based Decisions
Structured models enable automated
•
analysis like crack detection, clearance
checks, and quantity extraction for
informed decisions.

Industry Workflow Advancement
•

The industry is moving toward using
structured models for improved
accuracy, efficiency, and collaboration.

Design Intent vs.
Constructed
Reality

Gap Between Design and Reality
• Construction often deviates from the
original design due to field conditions
and unforeseen obstacles.

Importance of Real-Time
Decisions
• On-site decisions lead to variations,
highlighting the dynamic nature of
construction projects.

Value of Digital As-Builts
• Digital as-builts provide accurate
records capturing the constructed
reality beyond design documents.

Layering Contextual Data
•

Imagery, meshes, CAD features, and
field photos are layered onto the LiDAR
skeleton to provide comprehensive
context.

Enabling
Downstream Uses
and Reducing
Uncertainty

Unified Spatial Backbone
• Digital as-builts create a single spatial

source supporting design, construction,
and operational needs seamlessly.

Data Fusion for Accuracy
• Combining LiDAR, imagery, and field

data reduces uncertainty and enriches
project context effectively.

Improved Decision-Making
• Managing projects with integrated

spatial data reduces risks, unexpected
redesigns, and supports better
decisions.

Accuracy,
Repeatability,
Automation, and
Safety

Accuracy in Design Verification
LiDAR provides consistent geometric
•
data that ensures precise design and
verification processes.

Repeatability for Change
Detection
• Repeated LiDAR captures allow for

detailed comparison and detection of
changes over time.

Automation Streamlines
Workflow
• Clean LiDAR inputs enable automated
checks, faster modeling, and more
efficient project workflows.

Enhanced Safety for Field Crews
• Digital LiDAR captures reduce fieldwork
near live traffic, lowering risk for crews.

Indiana Signal Pole Case Study

Practical Examples and Scalable Approaches

LiDAR Workflows

• Three practical workflows demonstrating

fusion of LiDAR with imagery and 3D
meshes for diverse projects.

Solutions at Scale

• Workflows designed to be scaled or
tailored to fit different client needs,
budgets, and project scopes.

Quality Control

• Emphasizes simple QA/QC methods

and the use of phone-based LiDAR for
efficient daily engineering capture.

Leaning Pole Identified at Signal Modernization Site

Field Observation

Light pole was clearly leaning

Engineering assumption

There was a foundation deterioration or localized material failure

Location

SR 18/SR 15 modernization in Grant County, Indiana

Action

A structural and subsurface investigation took place.

GPR Deployed: Initial
Subsurface Indicators

Objective:

400 MHz antenna (~5–6 ft depth).

Identify
underground
voids and
vaulted
basements

Out-of-phase reflections indicate
concrete air boundary.

Signatures suggested vaulted
basement beneath sidewalk.

Plan View: Vaulted Basement Under the Sidewalk

1. Intersection corner show vaulted basements.

2. Vault extends beneath sidewalk toward curb.

3. Soil support missing under pole location.

Terrestrial LiDAR Confirms Why the Pole Was
Leaning

LiDAR revealed
the 3D
geometry of
the basement
under roadway.

Basement wall
aligns directly
beneath pole.

The Real Cause: Basement Extended to the Roadway

Not a pole hardware or isolated
foundation issue.

Historic basement construction
extended under sidewalk to curb.

Pole footing ended up positioned
over void causing lean.

PREVENTING HEAD-ON COLLISIONS IN WORK
ZONES WITH MOBILE MAPPING

INDIANA CASE STUDY OF A FATAL COLLISION

Fatal Work Zone Collision
• A 2025 head-on collision near Kokomo, Indiana caused a
fatality involving a construction worker and two vehicles.

Contributing Roadway Factors
• Narrow lanes, compressed traffic, and abrupt lane

changes created hazardous conditions leading to the
collision.

Worker Vulnerability
• Road workers are exposed to high risk due to proximity
to active lanes without adequate protection barriers.

Safety Technology Importance
• Enhanced monitoring technologies are crucial for early
detection of geometric or operational risks in work
zones.

GROWING RISK ENVIRONMENT IN WORK ZONES
Increasing Crash Risks
• Work zones in the US show rising crash rates

and fatalities, reaching 898 deaths and
101,000 crashes in 2023.

Challenges of Lane Geometry
• Narrow lanes and shifting alignments in work
zones complicate driver maneuverability,
increasing collisions.

Insufficient Physical Barriers
•

Temporary traffic control like cones and
lightweight barriers offer minimal protection
against vehicle intrusions.

Need for Advanced Safety Tech
•

Innovations like mobile mapping lane width
assessment can provide early detection.

Repurposing Scan Data and Point Clouds
for Monitoring - Case Study

Paul Tokarczyk
Survey Manager
LHB Corporation
Duluth, MN

Michael Powers
Senior Environmental Data Management Technician
Barr Engineering Co.
Duluth, MN

Outline

• Introductions

• Project History

• Equipment Used

• Data Collection/Limitations

• Point Cloud Repurposing

• Lessons Learned

Michael Powers

• 11 + years with Barr Engineering
• 6 years working in Reality Capture
• Terrestrial laser scanning
• UAS remote sensing (Part 107 Certification)
• Bathymetric data

collection

• GNSS and optical

survey

Paul Tokarczyk

• 28 years working in the civil/survey

consulting field

• PLS – MN; PE – MN, ND, WI, MI, NV,

•

OK
Instructor – Civil Engineering
Technology at Lake Superior College

What We Do

Project Site

• Fraser Shipyard LLC (Fraser)
• Located in the Duluth/Superior Port

in Superior, WI

• Site in operation since 1891

• Shipbuilding
• Certification
• Emergency Repair

• One of only 4 dry docks in the

Great Lakes

• 2 dry docks onsite
• Dry Dock 1 (DD1)
• Dry Dock 2 (DD2)

Project History

• Fraser docks U.S. Coast Guard (USCG) surface assets.

• Required Compliance with Surface Forces Logistics Center 8634 Specification (SFLC

8634)

• Bi-annual inspection by independent Professional Engineer

• Fraser hired Barr Engineering Co. (Barr) in 2019
• Barr designed a detailed inspection plan including:

• Electrical inspection
• Structural inspection

• Visual inspection of currently condition of interior and exterior dock components

•

3D terrestrial laser scan to monitor wall movement

DD1
• 2019
• 2021
• 2025

DD2
• 2020
• 2023
• 2025

Equipment

• Leica P40 Scan Station

• Leica RTC 360

Initial Workflow

• 3D scans collected of entire dry dock below grade

• Select locations scanned above grade

• Scans conducted in assumed coordinates

• Combination of target based and cloud-based registration

• Processing Software
• Cyclone Core
• Cyclone Register 360

Original Deliverables

• Autodesk Revit

Limitations of Evaluation Method

• Cross sections only provide small snapshot of dock wall for

comparison

• Movement could be occurring between cross-section

measurements

Limitations of Data Collection

• Unpredictable dock schedule

• Multiple Field Personnel

• Different equipment - P40 and RTC360

• Obstacles
• Debris
• Support cribbing
• Docked vessel
• Deeply corrugated sheet pile walls

• Long linear distance

Solution

• Entire picture of dock wall movement needed

• Compare all available data

• 400 – 500 million points, just within the extent of the dock

• But how?

Risk Mitigation

• “Can we do this…?” Yeah!… Maybe… We’ll try…

• Manage expectations of what can be done with the existing data

• Do the data collection methods meet the needs of the request

Full Dock Wall Comparison

• Clouds were not in a unified coordinate system

• Clouds loaded in Cyclone Register 360

• Common points identified across each scan for each dock

Scan Alignment (Register 360)

• User Coordinate System (UCS) applied to the baseline point

cloud with arbitrary origin (i.e.10000,1000,100)

• Coordinates extracted for the previously identified control points

• Coordinates (control) then applied to subsequent year clouds

Cloud Comparison Software

• TopoDOT and 3DReshaper/3DR were considered.

• TopoDOT learning curve too much for window of time.

• 3DReshaper was chosen.

• Available license
• Functionality
• Familiarity

Movement Analysis

• Data was exported from Register 360 in e57 format.

• Full clouds imported into 3DReshaper

• Point cloud cleaned and

trimmed

• Dock wall floor and upper

level removed

• Port and starboard walls

isolated

• Noise reduction

Movement Analysis (Cont.)

• Mesh generated to further reduce

noise

• Points extracted from mesh – 0.1 ft

avg. spacing

• 3D Inspection

• “Compare / Inspect” tool
• Colorized cloud generated

comparing most recent scan year
with the baseline

Lessons Learned

• Manage those expectations for everyone involved

• Think about and try to plan for future requests

• Common tie points or tied to a geodetic control system

• Working with old data requires time and effort

• Develop standardized workflow

• Field workflow
• Office workflow
• Provide adequate background and training for new project staff

Questions

Mike Powers

Paul Tokarczyk

Barr Engineering Co.

LHB Corp.

Mpowers@barr.com

Paul.Tokarczyk@LHBCorp.com
summary: The study focuses on repurposing scan data and point clouds for monitoring purposes, specifically using them to track movements in shipyard dry docks. The authors detail their methodology and tools used, highlighting challenges faced and solutions implemented.
